description: Boer OFS commandos cross the Orange and capture Aliwal North
source: Wikipedia: Jan Hendrik Olivier

description: Boers occupy Stormberg junction and range through Colesberg/Barkly East/Dordrecht districts
source: Wikipedia: Jan Hendrik Olivier
note: Regional invasion command above Olivier sometimes given as E.E. Grobler

description: Gatacre entrains his force to Molteno the nearest British-held railhead to Stormberg
source: britishbattles.com; historyofwar.org

description: Night march to storm the Kissieberg goes astray (lost guides + route change); British defeated
source: britishbattles.com; historyofwar.org; Wikipedia: Battle of Stormberg
note: British strength engaged was below 2600 (some detachments failed to arrive)

description: Boers hold the Kissieberg and repel Gatacre; one of the three Black Week defeats
source: britishbattles.com; Wikipedia: Battle of Stormberg
note: Boer strength disputed (~800 / 1700 / ~2300) - headcount NOT reliable

description: British retreat; 13 officers and 548 men captured plus killed/wounded; at least one gun lost
source: britishbattles.com; Wikipedia: Battle of Stormberg
note: Prisoner total 548 vs missing ~600-696 across sources; 1 vs 2 guns lost

description: First Cape invasion - long looping raid through the Karoo/Midlands; exits near Bethulie 29 Apr 1901
source: SAMHS vol017ng; Wikipedia: P.H. Kritzinger
note: Start strength & exact first-crossing date REFUTED/uncertain; force split at Maraisburg

description: Enters with Kritzinger then wide-ranging Midlands guerrilla operations until capture
source: SAMHS jnl2 vol185gs; Wikipedia: Gideon Scheepers

description: Scheepers captured (later tried and executed)
source: Wikipedia: Gideon Scheepers; SAMHS jnl2 vol185gs
note: Date given as ~10-12 Oct 1901

description: Second invasion with reinforcements; route partly aggregates a 3-way commando split
source: SAMHS vol017ng; Wikipedia: P.H. Kritzinger
note: Re-entry date 16 May (SAMHS) vs 19 May (Wikipedia); Tarkastad leg was Lotter's

description: Kritzinger's commando captures Jamestown after a fight
source: SAMHS vol017ng; Wikipedia: P.H. Kritzinger

description: Kritzinger wounded & captured charging the Noupoort-De Aar blockhouse line in daylight
source: SAMHS vol017ng; Wikipedia: P.H. Kritzinger
note: Physical capture credited to 3rd Bn Grenadier Guards; Doran directing commander

description: Smuts's commando transits the eastern Cape; bypasses Jansenville's armed Town Guard fort
source: SAMHS vol102rt
note: Single local-history source; Jansenville/Aberdeen leg dated Nov 1901

description: Four columns sweep the Camdeboo after Scheepers; a laager near Aberdeen is surprised 15 Aug (~30 taken)
source: SAMHS vol073vm

description: Crabbe's column brushes Fouche (May) and Kritzinger (July); falls back on Mortimer 21 Jul
source: Wikipedia: Eyre Crabbe; SAMHS vol073vm

description: Gorringe routs Kritzinger's commando near Steynsburg; Erasmus and Cachet taken
source: Wikipedia: George Gorringe; SAMHS vol073vm

description: Lotter splits from Kritzinger (~Mar) and raids the Cape Midlands until destroyed
source: SAMHS vol015hk; Wikipedia: Johannes Lotter

description: Lotter's commando captures a party of French's Scouts near Bethesda Road
source: SAMHS vol015hk

description: Doran's column strikes Lotter at Garstland Kloof near Cradock
source: SAMHS vol015hk; Wikipedia: Beauchamp Doran

description: Scobell destroys Lotter's commando at Groenkloof; Boer ~19 killed / ~114 captured
source: SAMHS vol015hk; Wikipedia: Battle of Groenkloof
note: Casualty counts & exact farm (Groenkloof vs Bouwershoek/Tandjesberg) source-conflicted

description: Lotter tried under martial law and executed at Middelburg ~12 Oct 1901
source: Wikipedia: Johannes Lotter; SAMHS vol015hk
note: 11 Oct date REFUTED (0-3); 12 Oct the likely date

description: Maritz raids the western Cape/Namaqualand; Leliefontein mission attack 31 Jan 1902
source: SAMHS vol201gv; Davitt
note: WESTERN Cape - outside Eastern Cape focus (included for completeness)

commander: Asst-Cmdt-Gen Jan Smuts & Cmdt van Deventer
description: Smuts led ~300 burghers from the Orange River into the western Cape Sep 1901, routing C Squadron 17th Lancers at Modderfontein, raiding as far as Springbok and Namaqualand. Besieged Okiep Apr-May 1902. The most daring Boer commando operation in the Cape Colony.
source: SAMHS vol201gv; Wikipedia
note: WESTERN Cape - outside Eastern Cape focus; van Deventer was a Commandant not a general

description: Van Reenen leads ~300 men (half a commando) back north across the Orange River
source: SAMHS vol073vm
note: Approx date (March 1901); part of the first-invasion northward retreat

description: Night attack on van Reenen's camp at Ruigtevlei (betrayed by a loyalist farmer); ~18 rebels captured; Jan Kruger + 1 British soldier killed; van Reenen & Capt Botha escape; Veldkornet Jan Kruger killed
source: news24 'Lootjie bepaal lot'; Conan Doyle ch.35; angloboerwar 17th Lancers
note: Conan Doyle dates it 6 Jun w/ 22 prisoners; Afrikaans source 7 Jun w/ 18 captured; Jan & Christiaan Kruger were veldkornets (family record)

description: Gorringe pursues the commandos from Steynsburg to Venterstad killing ~20 and taking prisoners
source: Conan Doyle ch.35; SAMHS vol073vm
note: Follows the 13 Aug rout of Kritzinger near Steynsburg (row 17)

description: Smuts overwhelms C Squadron 17th Lancers (Capt Sandeman) at Modderfontein; Boer victory
source: Wikipedia: Battle of Elands River (1901)
note: British 29 killed/41 wounded/60 captured; Boer 1 killed/6 wounded; Smuts re-arms from captured kit

description: Five rebels of van Reenen's commando condemned; lots drawn (one to be shot as example) - the lot fell on Kloppert
source: news24 'Lootjie bepaal lot'
note: The five: Kloppert; Andries & Christiaan Kruger; C.W.C. van Biljon; Stef Koekemoer

description: Petrus Kloppert executed at Burgersdorp as a deterrent; the other four condemned (incl. both Krugers) exiled to Bermuda
source: news24 'Lootjie bepaal lot'
note: Execution method (shot vs hanged) conflicts across sources

description: Kritzinger's men overwhelm a ~25-man British detachment at Klipplaat
source: angloboerwar Cape chronology

description: An Imperial Yeomanry patrol is surprised and captured near Willowmore
source: angloboerwar Cape chronology

description: Kritzinger engages Gorringe's column at Fish River Station north of Cradock
source: angloboerwar Cape chronology
note: anchored near Cradock

description: Malan's commando tries to occupy Richmond but is repulsed
source: angloboerwar Cape chronology

description: Kritzinger surrounds the village of Pearston
source: angloboerwar Cape chronology

description: Kritzinger derails a train on the Cradock line (8 Apr) and loots Conway (9 Apr)
source: angloboerwar Cape chronology
note: Conway anchored on the Cradock-Middelburg line

description: 17th Lancers enter Cape Colony (Dec 1900); split into column squadrons from June 1901
source: angloboerwar 17th Lancers; Wikipedia
note: date_end cleared: was 1901-06-30; the Jun 30 end caused t_start > t_end reversal vs Cradock engagement Feb 23 1901 (ID 35)

description: Gorringe's column pursues Smuts southward after the Modderfontein action
source: angloboerwar Cape chronology
note: direction of pursuit; anchored at Tarkastad

description: Columns under Parsons & Scobell relieve the Aberdeen garrison attacked by Scheepers; IY patrols lose 4 killed
source: SAMHS vol141sw; saartillery IY

description: Brabant's Horse 1st corps heavily engaged near Richmond vs Malan (May-June Cradock/Richmond ops)
source: angloboerwar Brabant's Horse

description: Nesbitt's Horse (Lt Callaghan) repulses a Boer attack at Ganna Hoek
source: angloboerwar Nesbitt's Horse; Kitchener despatch 8 Dec 1901
note: Ganna Hoek anchored in the Bankberg/Cradock district

description: Local troops defeat and capture Cmdt Wynand Malan near Sheldon, ~4 days before peace
source: angloboerwar Jansenville DMT; SAMHS vol073vm

description: C Squadron railed from Stormberg to Tarkastad on 15 Sep then posted forward to van Heerden's farm Modderfontein (~22km NW of Tarkastad) to guard the Buysrivierpoort and Elandsrivierpoort passes; overrun by Smuts on 17 Sep
source: SAMHS vol131rs; Wikipedia: Battle of Elands River (1901)
note: C Sqn camp dominated by high ground; high Elands River prevented withdrawal

description: Haig held his HQ and two further Lancer squadrons at Tarkastad (A Squadron camped forward at Hoogstede); on news of the attack at 16:30 on 17 Sep Haig galloped ~22km to Modderfontein, arriving too late to pursue
source: SAMHS vol131rs
note: the missing Tarkastad leg of the 17th Lancers

description: Modderfontein wounded carried back into Tarkastad and on to the Red Cross hospital at Cradock
source: SAMHS vol131rs

description: Fouché crossed into the Cape with Hertzog's Dec-1900 invasion (alongside Kritzinger and Scheepers) and ranged the Cradock-Maraisburg-Steynsburg-Middelburg districts in early Jan 1901
source: SAMHS vol073vm; bwm.org.au
note: entered with Hertzog/Kritzinger/Scheepers/Smith

commander: Scheepers, Cmdt G.; Cmdt W. Fouché
description: After splitting from Kritzinger mid-Feb, Scheepers & Fouché skirmish with Col L.W. Parsons' column and are forced north out of the Willowmore district
source: SAMHS vol073vm

commander: Scheepers, Cmdt G.; Cmdt W. Fouché
description: Scheepers & Fouché attack Aberdeen but are repulsed by the town guard and retreat into the Koudeveld mountains (Boer side of the 5-6 Mar Aberdeen relief, row 41)
source: SAMHS vol073vm
note: corroborates row 41 from the Boer perspective

description: Scheepers clashes with colonial scouts at Graaff-Reinet; the farm Uitkomst destroyed
source: SAMHS jnl2 vol185gs

description: Scheepers engages colonial troops at Jansenville
source: SAMHS jnl2 vol185gs

description: Scheepers derails a train at Mortimer and loots the stores at Pearston
source: SAMHS jnl2 vol185gs
note: railway-wrecking phase in the Midlands

description: Scheepers engaged at Gannahoek near Cradock
source: SAMHS jnl2 vol185gs

description: Scheepers clashes at Quaggashoek near Tarkastad
source: SAMHS jnl2 vol185gs
note: another Tarkastad-district action

description: Scheepers ambushes and burns a train at Marais Siding on the midland railway
source: SAMHS jnl2 vol185gs
note: Marais Siding anchored on the Midland line near Cradock

description: Scheepers occupies Murraysburg three times (14 & 27 Jun and 6 Jul); public buildings and shops destroyed
source: SAMHS jnl2 vol185gs
note: occupied three separate times

description: Scheepers engaged in the Camdeboo Mountains as French's four columns sweep the district
source: SAMHS jnl2 vol185gs; SAMHS vol073vm
note: coincides with the four-column drive (row 15)

description: Scheepers engaged at Rooiklip/Wanhoop near Steytlerville before drifting south-west out of the Eastern Cape
source: SAMHS jnl2 vol185gs
note: last major Eastern-Cape action before the SW Karoo drift

description: The day after the repulse at Richmond Malan captures a patrol of Kitchener's Fighting Scouts
source: SAMHS vol073vm

description: Malan suffers a severe reverse at Blaauwkrantz - 14 Boers killed and 4 taken prisoner (3 British killed)
source: SAMHS vol073vm; SAMHS vol102rt
note: Blaauwkrantz placed 20km N of Jansenville per the Town Guard forts study; date 20 Mar (vol073vm) vs 21 Mar (vol102rt)

description: Malan captures 8 men of the Diamond Fields Horse near Cradock (2 May) and blows up the line at Mortimer south of Cradock (2-3 May)
source: SAMHS vol073vm

description: Malan is driven north into the Richmond district by pursuing columns
source: SAMHS vol073vm

description: Malan again attacks Richmond (Brabant's Horse heavily engaged nearby on 28 Jun - row 43)
source: SAMHS vol073vm

description: Volunteers form a Town Guard at Jansenville (supplemented by British troops) under Capt Gould; a fort is built in Jan 1901
source: SAMHS vol102rt; angloboerwar Jansenville TG

description: The Pearston Town Guard is overwhelmed when Kritzinger surrounds and loots the village (3-6 Mar 1901)
source: SAMHS vol073vm; angloboerwar
note: Boer side recorded in row 36

description: The Aberdeen Town Guard repulses the attack by Scheepers & Fouché (6 Mar 1901)
source: SAMHS vol073vm
note: Boer side in rows 41 & 51

description: Scheepers attacks Willowmore for the second time; six of the Town Guard are killed defending the village
source: SAMHS vol094dd; SAMHS jnl2 vol185gs
note: dedicated SAMHS study of the 1 Jun 1901 attack

description: The Tarkastad Mounted Troops, with Maj H.T. Nickalls of the 17th Lancers attached, see action near Vaal Vlei on 8 Oct 1901
source: angloboerwar Boer War Forum (Town Guard/DMT thread); angloboerwar locally-raised troops

commander: Gardner, Cmdt J.; Rubidge, Adj Capt F.
description: Graaff-Reinet raises a Town Guard of 100 men under Cmdt J. Gardner (Adjutant Capt F. Rubidge); strength grows to 220 by Mar 1902
source: SAMHS vol073vm

description: Kritzinger's main body cuts off a strong British patrol of ~100 Lancers and Yeomanry north of Aberdeen, taking 75 temporary prisoners
source: Conan Doyle ch.32; SAMHS vol073vm
note: attribution conflict: Conan Doyle credits Kritzinger (7 Apr); SAMHS vol073vm credits Scheepers at Zeekoegat farm near Aberdeen (6 Apr) - likely the same 75-prisoner action

description: Kritzinger crosses the Orange ~5 miles west of Odendaalstroom on the night of 15 Dec 1900 with ~300 men and clashes with Grenfell's troops near Venterstad on 19 Dec
source: SAMHS vol017ng; cf. Conan Doyle ch.32
note: crossing point/strength conflict: Odendaalstroom 15 Dec ~300 (vol017ng) vs Rhenoster Hoek 16 Dec ~800 then via Burgersdorp (Conan Doyle); opening move of 1st invasion

description: Kritzinger (with Malan) surprises the Midland Mounted Rifles at Waterkloof and badly mauls them: 10 killed, 11 wounded, 66 prisoners
source: Conan Doyle ch.35; SAMHS vol017ng; SAMHS vol073vm
note: date 20 Jun (Conan Doyle) vs 21 Jun (SAMHS); 'near Swaershoek' (vol073vm) vs '30mi W of Cradock' (Conan Doyle); merges two former duplicate rows

description: On his last re-entry (crossed 11 Dec with ~101 men) Kritzinger attacks Lovat's Scouts at Quaggafontein and is attacked at Colesberg before being trapped at Fransmanskop on 16 Dec (row 12)
source: SAMHS vol017ng
note: leads directly into the capture at Fransmanskop

description: Malan overwhelms a strong patrol of the Midland Mounted Rifles south of Maraisburg: 6 killed, 11 wounded, 41 prisoners, capturing rifles and ammunition
source: Conan Doyle ch.35
note: distinct from the 20 Jun Waterkloof mauling (row 76)

description: Scobell and Lukin surprise a laager in the Barkly East district, taking 20 prisoners, 166 horses and much of the Jamestown loot
source: Conan Doyle ch.35

description: Monro with Lovat's Scouts and Bethune's Mounted Infantry has a minor success against the commandos near Tarkastad
source: Conan Doyle ch.35
note: another Tarkastad-district action

description: Fouché dashes from Barkly East into the Transkei reserves for horses and supplies but is turned back north by Griqualand Mounted Rifles and Dalgety's colonials
source: Conan Doyle ch.35
note: approximate dates (end of June)

description: Fouché strongly attacks a Connaught Rangers convoy under Major Moore near Jamestown but is driven off after hours of fighting (British 7 killed, 17 wounded)
source: Conan Doyle ch.35

description: Lukin with 90 men rides into 150 of Lategan's band near Murraysburg, capturing 10 men and 100 horses
source: Conan Doyle ch.35
note: first appearance of Lategan's commando in the data

description: French's cordon hustles the main Boer body from Graaff-Reinet to Theebus (7-10 Aug) and thrusts it over the Stormberg-Naauwpoort railway line with loss of men and horses
source: Conan Doyle ch.35

description: Kritzinger's force crosses the railway at Sherburne north of Rosmead Junction and holds up a train carrying Colonial troops
source: Conan Doyle ch.32; SAMHS jnl2 vol196rs
note: first railway smash of the invasion

description: Byng's column has a sharp skirmish with Kritzinger's men near Murraysburg at a cost of ~20 casualties
source: Conan Doyle ch.32

description: The Boer vanguard dashes ~70 miles in two days - at Aberdeen on 16 Jan and Willowmore on 18 Jan - Scheepers making his first attack on Willowmore on 19 Jan
source: Conan Doyle ch.32; SAMHS jnl2 vol196rs

description: Boers (~200) overwhelm Capt Oliver's 25 men of the 7th Dragoon Guards and West Australians at Klipplaat after an 8-hour defence costing the patrol ~50% casualties
source: Conan Doyle ch.32

description: Kritzinger falls back to Bethesda with Gorringe and Lowe's columns at his heels
source: Conan Doyle ch.32

description: OFS commandos cross at Norval's Pont in November 1899 and occupy the Colesberg district (~2,000 men), threatening the midland railway and Lord Methuen's communications
source: With the Flag to Pretoria vol.1 ch.XV

description: French concentrates around Arundel/Rensburg to contain the Colesberg Boers and on 13 Dec repels a ~1,800-strong Boer attack near Arundel and Kuilfontein (Boers ~40 casualties; British 9)
source: With the Flag to Pretoria vol.1 ch.XV

description: The Boers fall back from Rensburg to Colesberg (29 Dec); French advances the camp to Rensburg seizing Porter's Hill and Maeder's Farm and the Berkshires rush a kopje by Coles Kop on 1 Jan
source: With the Flag to Pretoria vol.1 ch.XV

description: Genl Schoeman's night attempt to outflank the British left is trapped and repulsed: Boer ~90 casualties and 21 prisoners; Maj Harvey (10th Hussars) killed
source: With the Flag to Pretoria vol.1 ch.XV

description: A night attack by the Suffolks on Grassy/Suffolk Hill is repulsed: A & H Companies (~200 men) mostly killed, wounded or captured and Lt-Col Watson killed
source: With the Flag to Pretoria vol.1 ch.XV

description: The British occupy and hold Slingersfontein SE of Colesberg through January, repelling Boer attacks (noted for the marksmanship of the Australian and New Zealand troops)
source: With the Flag to Pretoria vol.1 ch.XV

description: French is summoned to lead the Kimberley relief (29 Jan) and Clements takes over; weakened, the British abandon Coles Kop and Slingersfontein and fall back on Arundel while the Boers reoccupy Colesberg
source: With the Flag to Pretoria vol.1 ch.XV

description: After Kritzinger's capture on 16 Dec 1901 the bulk of his commando escapes south and infests the Cradock and Somerset East districts into early 1902
source: Handbook of the Boer War (IV Cape Colony)

description: Fouché's band, long lurking in the north-east between Dordrecht and Aliwal North, pushes into the Midlands in early 1902 and links up with Malan (~18 Mar)
source: Handbook of the Boer War (IV Cape Colony); SAMHS vol073vm

description: French launches 'the Drive' north from Beaufort West (17 Feb 1902); it disperses but does not destroy the rebels, some of whom scrape round the flank, cross the railway and plunge into the Graaff-Reinet and Aberdeen districts
source: Handbook of the Boer War (IV Cape Colony)

description: Cmdt Henry Hugo is killed near Three Sisters Station as Malan's group tries to recross the railway into the Midlands; only Malan and Rudolph get across (Hugo replaced by Rudolph)
source: SAMHS vol073vm
note: western-Karoo leg of the van Reenen/Malan return

description: British garrisons evacuate Naauwpoort (2 Nov) and Stormberg (3 Nov) to Sterkstroom/Queenstown; Gatacre arrives 18 Nov, reconnoitres Bushman's Hoek (21 Nov) and pushes to Putter's Kraal
source: With the Flag to Pretoria vol.1 ch.VII
note: the build-up to the Stormberg battle

description: A commando of ~1,500 Boers advances from Burgersdorp and seizes Stormberg Junction, cutting Gatacre off from the De Aar force
source: With the Flag to Pretoria vol.1 ch.VII
note: refines the date of the Stormberg occupation (row 2)

description: A Boer commando of about 500 men occupies Dordrecht north-east of Sterkstroom
source: With the Flag to Pretoria vol.1 ch.VII

description: The 17th Lancers disembark at Cape Town c.10 March 1900 (sailed on the SS Victorian from England on 14 Feb: 22 officers, 497 men, 468 horses)
source: angloboerwar 17th Lancers; ABW shipping records
note: arrival in theatre

description: Railed up-country from Cape Town via De Aar to join Lord Roberts at Bloemfontein in Gordon's 3rd Cavalry Brigade (with the 9th & 16th Lancers); fought at Diamond Hill (Jun 1900) before later coming south into the Cape Colony
source: angloboerwar 17th Lancers
note: logistical rail move into the interior

description: Join Lord Roberts' main northern advance in French's 3rd Cavalry Brigade (with the 9th & 16th Lancers), advancing from Bloemfontein to the occupation of Pretoria (5 Jun 1900)
source: angloboerwar 17th Lancers
note: Transvaal/OFS leg of the journey

description: Roberts attacked Botha's line east of Pretoria at Diamond Hill (Donkerhoek) 11-12 Jun 1900. After a day's fighting the Boers withdrew overnight; British occupied the position. Last major engagement before Botha dispersed into guerrilla warfare.
source: angloboerwar 17th Lancers; Wikipedia: Battle of Diamond Hill
note: Transvaal

description: Formed into a column under Col Herbert and operating under Col Barker in the south-east Orange River Colony, the 17th Lancers join the pursuit of De Wet as he tries to break into the Cape Colony (Dec 1900)
source: angloboerwar 17th Lancers
note: leads into their entry to the Cape Colony

description: A two-month interlude back in the Orange River Colony under Col Williams (Apr-May 1901) before returning to the Cape Colony for the rest of the war
source: angloboerwar 17th Lancers

description: Brabant's colonial division advances from Penhoek and occupies Dordrecht on 18 Feb 1900 after a spirited action
source: Conan Doyle ch.22
note: opens the NE-Cape colonial advance

description: Brabant drives the Boers out of the Labuschagne's Nek position north of Dordrecht after a long day's fighting
source: Conan Doyle ch.22

description: Leaving a garrison at Dordrecht, Brabant pushes on with ~2,000 men and eight guns and occupies Jamestown
source: Conan Doyle ch.22

description: Maj Henderson seizes the Aliwal North bridge before it can be blown; a Boer stand beyond the bridge costs ~25 casualties
source: Conan Doyle ch.22

description: Brabant's detachments pacify the north-eastern Cape - Barkly East, Herschel and Lady Grey - and push into the SE Free State via Rouxville
source: Conan Doyle ch.22

description: Gatacre re-occupies Molteno and reconnoitres the Stormberg position; Capt de Montmorency is killed in the reconnaissance
source: Conan Doyle ch.21

description: As the Boers retreat Gatacre reoccupies Stormberg (5 Mar), pushes to Burgersdorp (12 Mar) and crosses into the OFS at Bethulie (15 Mar); Cape Police save the Bethulie road bridge
source: Conan Doyle ch.21-22

description: De la Rey's reinforced Boers counter-attack along the Colesberg line; the British abandon Coles Kop and Slingersfontein and fall back
source: With the Flag to Pretoria vol.1 ch.XV; Conan Doyle ch.14
note: the Boer counter that forced Clements back

description: After the Boers retire north Clements advances from Arundel and re-occupies Colesberg (28 Feb), then crosses the Orange at Norvalspont (4 Mar)
source: Conan Doyle ch.21
note: end of the Colesberg campaign

description: A spirited Boer attack on New Zealand Hill at Slingersfontein is repulsed by New Zealanders and 1st Yorkshires with the bayonet; ~21 Boer dead
source: With the Flag to Pretoria vol.1 ch.XV; Conan Doyle ch.14

description: Scheepers skirmishes near Middelburg at Grootvlei; one British soldier killed
source: SAMHS jnl2 vol185gs

description: Scheepers brushes the Bedford Town Guard (7 May) and clashes with Col Henniker's column near Bedford (8 May)
source: SAMHS vol073vm
note: first appearance of Henniker's column

description: Scheepers loots a train at Ganna Siding; three soldiers killed
source: SAMHS jnl2 vol185gs
note: anchored in the Cradock/Bankberg district

description: Smuts's commando is briefly cornered atop the Stormberg mountain while pushing south, four days before Modderfontein
source: morethanastory.co.za 'Guerrillas in the Cape'

description: Van Deventer overwhelms a ~50-man post at Doornbosch and captures the relieving 130 Somerset East District Defence Force with 220 horses
source: SAMHS vol073vm; Davitt diary

description: Col Lukin surprises Van Deventer's commando at Steilhoogte; 14 captured and 60 horses/mules taken
source: SAMHS vol073vm

description: Cmdt Bouwer clashes with the District Defence Force at Springvale and captures 20 men
source: SAMHS vol073vm

description: Wessels (Kritzinger's successor) enters Murraysburg with ~20 men in early Jan 1902 and is welcomed by the inhabitants
source: SAMHS vol073vm

description: Cmdt Wessels with ~200 invaders captures 50 of the Cradock Town Guard who had ridden out to find the Boers
source: Davitt diary

description: Malan and Fouché help Van Heerden attack Aberdeen; many horses are taken but Van Heerden is killed
source: SAMHS vol073vm

description: Fouché surrenders in the Cradock district on learning the war has ended (Peace of Vereeniging, 31 May 1902)
source: SAMHS vol073vm; Conan Doyle ch.35
note: one of the last Cape commandos to lay down arms

description: The 9th Lancers (from Muttra, India) disembark at Cape Town c.12 Oct 1899 off the transport Nairung
source: Naval & Military Press regimental diary; ABW shipping records
note: arrival from India

description: As Methuen's only cavalry the 9th Lancers work round the Boer rear at the Battle of Belmont on the Kimberley line
source: britishbattles.com Battle of Belmont

description: The 9th Lancers and mounted infantry guard the right flank at the Battle of Modder River
source: bwm.org.au Modder River

description: The 9th Lancers are engaged at Magersfontein during Methuen's advance on Kimberley
source: Wikipedia: 9th Queen's Royal Lancers

description: With French's cavalry division (Gordon's 3rd Brigade) the 9th Lancers ride in the final dash that relieves Kimberley
source: historyofwar.org Relief of Kimberley

description: Roberts encircled Cronje's 4,000-man laager on the Modder River 18 Feb 1900. After Kitchener's costly frontal assault on 18 Feb (Paardeberg Drift), the Boers were starved into surrender on 27 Feb – Majuba Day – opening the road to Bloemfontein.
source: Wikipedia: Battle of Paardeberg

description: 235 CMR with mountain guns were to join Gatacre's Stormberg attack from Penhoek but never received the order
source: britishbattles.com Battle of Stormberg

description: The CMR form the core of the colonial garrison that holds Jammersbergdrift/Wepener through De Wet's 17-day siege
source: OMSA Siege of Wepener
note: just over the NE-Cape border in the OFS

description: Brabant raises the colonial light-horse regiment Brabant's Horse from eastern-Cape volunteers (max ~600 all ranks)
source: Wikipedia: Edward Brabant
note: raised locally - no overseas disembarkation

description: 1st and 2nd Brabant's Horse move north and help hold Wepener through the 17-day siege by De Wet
source: OMSA Siege of Wepener

description: The 1st-contingent Imperial Yeomanry disembark at Cape Town (Feb-Apr 1900) and camp at Maitland
source: SAMHS vol136sw

description: The IY's first action: 3rd & 10th battalions engage Villebois-Mareuil's foreign volunteers at Tweefontein near Boshof
source: SAMHS vol136sw

description: The 13th Battalion IY is surrounded and forced to surrender at Lindley (the Lindley affair)
source: SAMHS vol136sw

description: French and Hamilton routed Kock's commando at Elandslaagte 21 Oct 1899. Gordon Highlanders and Imperial Light Horse broke the Boer position; 5th Lancers pursued. One of few clear British set-piece victories early in the war; General Kock mortally wounded.
source: Wikipedia: 5th Royal Irish Lancers
note: no 1899 disembarkation - in SA since 1898

description: The 5th Lancers form part of the besieged garrison through the Siege of Ladysmith
source: Wikipedia: 5th Royal Irish Lancers

description: 1st Coldstream Guards arrive at the Cape c.16 Nov 1899 and join Methuen's Guards Brigade
source: britishbattles.com Belmont

description: The Guards Brigade fights at Belmont (23 Nov) and Modder River (28 Nov) on the western railway
source: britishbattles.com Belmont & Modder River

description: 1st Scots Guards arrive at the Cape c.13 Nov 1899 and fight with the Guards Brigade at Belmont (23 Nov)
source: britishbattles.com Belmont

description: The CIV disembark at Cape Town (Jan-Feb 1900) and camp at Green Point
source: Wikipedia: City of London Imperial Volunteers

description: CIV Mounted Infantry come under fire for the first time at Jacobsdal during the Kimberley relief operations
source: Wikipedia: City of London Imperial Volunteers

description: The CIV battalion rails up from De Aar and Orange River to join the advance toward Bloemfontein
source: Wikipedia: City of London Imperial Volunteers

description: The 1st Royal Scots land at East London (~late Nov 1899) and serve in the Queenstown district under Gatacre
source: Royal Scots regimental record

description: ~1,250 strong, the Royal Scots concentrate at Belfast in time for the fighting before Bergendal (27 Aug 1900)
source: Royal Scots regimental record

description: Prince Alfred's Guard mobilises (16 Oct 1899) to garrison Port Elizabeth and guard the Cape Town-Orange River railway
source: Prince Alfred's Guard record
note: a Port Elizabeth volunteer corps

description: The mounted squadrons of Prince Alfred's Guard serve under Clements about Arundel, repelling a Boer attack on 20 Feb
source: Prince Alfred's Guard record
note: Colesberg front

description: Penn Symons's brigade stormed Boer-held Talana Hill at Dundee 20 Oct 1899. Costly British infantry attack drove the Boers off the summit; Penn Symons was mortally wounded during the advance. First pitched battle of the war on Natal soil.
source: historyofwar.org

description: French and Hamilton routed Kock's commando at Elandslaagte 21 Oct 1899. Gordon Highlanders and Imperial Light Horse broke the Boer position; 5th Lancers pursued. One of few clear British set-piece victories early in the war; General Kock mortally wounded.
source: Wikipedia: Battle of Elandslaagte

description: White's holding action covering the Dundee garrison's retreat into Ladysmith
source: historyofwar.org

description: 'Mournful Monday' - White's sortie fails; a column is lost at Nicholson's Nek and the army falls back into Ladysmith
source: historyofwar.org

description: Boer forces under Joubert invested Ladysmith 2 Nov 1899, trapping White's 12,000-man garrison. 118-day siege ended 28 Feb 1900 when Buller's Tugela breakthrough opened the relief road. Pivotal Natal campaign action.
source: historyofwar.org

description: Joubert's force checks a British attack south of Estcourt; the British withdraw
source: Wikipedia: Battle of Willow Grange

description: Buller's frontal assault on Botha's Tugela line fails disastrously, with the loss of ten guns - the climax of Black Week
source: britishbattles.com

commander: Sir Redvers Buller; Sir Charles Warren
description: Buller and Warren seized Spion Kop summit 23-24 Jan 1900 but the crest was dominated by Boer fire from surrounding heights. Thorneycroft held through the night then abandoned the summit. British ~1,700 casualties; a defining Black Week failure.
source: britishbattles.com

description: Buller's third attempt to force the Tugela; the cramped bridgehead is abandoned after three days
source: Wikipedia: Battle of Vaal Krantz

description: Sustained assaults finally break Botha's Tugela line on 27 Feb, opening the road to Ladysmith
source: Wikipedia: Battle of the Tugela Heights

description: Buller's relief column enters Ladysmith, ending the 118-day siege
source: Wikipedia: Relief of Ladysmith

description: Methuen again drives the Boers from the kopjes at Graspan, continuing his advance up the railway
source: Wikipedia: Battle of Graspan

description: Cronje's concealed trenches shatter the Highland Brigade's dawn attack - the Black Week defeat that halts the Kimberley relief
source: Wikipedia: Battle of Magersfontein

description: A colonial flying column overruns a Boer laager near Douglas - first action for Australian and Canadian troops
source: bwm.org.au

commander: Roberts, Lord; Kitchener, Lord; Kelly-Kenny, T.
description: Roberts encircled Cronje's 4,000-man laager on the Modder River 18 Feb 1900. After Kitchener's costly frontal assault on 18 Feb (Paardeberg Drift), the Boers were starved into surrender on 27 Feb – Majuba Day – opening the road to Bloemfontein.
source: historyofwar.org

description: Piet Cronje surrendered ~4,000 burghers to Roberts at Paardeberg on 27 Feb 1900 – deliberately chosen as the anniversary of Majuba Hill. Largest Boer capitulation to that date; decisive blow to the conventional Boer war effort.
source: britishbattles.com

description: Roberts attacks De Wet's line; the Boers (with President Kruger present) abandon it before the flanking cavalry
source: historyofwar.org

description: De Wet and De la Rey hold all day but are dislodged after dark - the last obstacle before Bloemfontein
source: historyofwar.org

description: Roberts occupies the Orange Free State capital after the great flank march
source: historyofwar.org

description: De Wet's ambush at the Bloemfontein waterworks captures guns, wagons and prisoners from Broadwood's column
source: Wikipedia: Sanna's Post

description: Roberts occupies Johannesburg as his army drives into the Transvaal
source: sahistory.org.za

description: Roberts enters Pretoria; the Boer government has already withdrawn eastward
source: sahistory.org.za

description: De Wet with Steyn and ~2,600 burghers broke out through unguarded Slabbert's Nek night of 15 Jul 1900, escaping Hunter's encirclement in the Brandwater Basin. ~4,300 men under Prinsloo were left behind and surrendered 30 Jul; De Wet's escape kept the OFS resistance alive.
source: SAMHS vol113hk

description: Buller broke the last Boer conventional defensive line at Bergendal kopje near Belfast 27 Aug 1900. A Zarps (ZARP police) detachment held the kopje until overwhelmed; Botha's line collapsed. Final set-piece battle of the conventional war.
source: Wikipedia: Battle of Bergendal

description: De Wet and Liebenberg besiege Barton's column on the Potchefstroom railway until relief breaks through on 25 Oct
source: battletoursza.com

description: British MI surprise De Wet's laager at dawn - a rare defeat for De Wet; Le Gallais mortally wounded
source: Wikipedia: Battle of Bothaville

commander: Gen. Koos de la Rey; Beyers, Christiaan
description: De la Rey and Beyers surprised Clements's isolated brigade at the foot of the Magaliesberg 13 Dec 1900. Boers descended the cliffs at dawn; British lost ~600 men killed, wounded or captured plus all camp stores. Clements extricated a remnant; a serious reverse in the guerrilla phase.
source: Wikipedia: Battle of Nooitgedacht

description: De Wet crosses into the Cape (11 Feb) seeking a general rising but, hampered by floods and pursuit, loses his guns and re-crosses the Orange
source: bwm.org.au De Wet hunt

description: Hertzog's commando seizes Calvinia during the first Boer thrust deep into the north-western Cape
source: sahistory.org.za

description: De la Rey routed Lord Methuen's column at Tweebosch (Harts River) 7 Mar 1902. Mounted Boers stampeded Methuen's horses then charged; Methuen was wounded and captured. Last major Boer field success; Methuen later released on parole.
source: Wikipedia: Battle of Tweebosch

description: Smuts's force takes Springbok in the western copper district before turning on Okiep
source: SAMHS vol165vz

description: Smuts led ~300 burghers from the Orange River into the western Cape Sep 1901, routing C Squadron 17th Lancers at Modderfontein, raiding as far as Springbok and Namaqualand. Besieged Okiep Apr-May 1902. The most daring Boer commando operation in the Cape Colony.
source: SAMHS vol165vz

description: Kemp led a 2,000-strong mounted Boer charge at Rooiwal (near Klerksdorp) 11 Apr 1902. Entrenched British infantry and artillery shattered the attack; ~200 Boers killed. The last massed offensive action of the war, ending Boer hope of a decisive battlefield reversal.
source: Wikipedia: Battle of Rooiwal

description: Warren's Division departed Ladysmith on 6 March 1900, sent by Roberts round by way of East London to join Gatacre's central advance, removing a large force from the Natal Army.
source: Handbook ch.XIII p.263

description: De Wet with 2,000 men and four guns surrounded a British column of 550 retreating from Dewetsdorp toward Reddersburg; after a day's resistance the column surrendered on 4 April, losing all men as prisoners.
source: Handbook ch.XII p.202â€“203

description: De Wet besieged Wepener with ten guns and 6,000 men for sixteen days; the garrison of ~1,900 under Dalgety held out until relieved by Brabant and Hart columns arriving from Aliwal North on 25 April.
source: Handbook ch.XII p.203â€“204

description: Buller launched a turning movement against the Boer Biggarsberg position beginning 11 May 1900; Clery's IInd Division swung east toward the Boer left flank at Helpmakaar, rendering the position untenable by 13 May.
source: Handbook ch.XIII p.265

force: Natal Army â€” Dundonald's mounted infantry
units: Natal Army â€” Dundonald's mounted infantry
description: After the Boers evacuated Helpmakaar under cover of veld fire, Dundonald's mounted infantry pushed through flames at dawn; Dundee was found clear and occupied the following day.
source: Handbook ch.XIII p.265

description: Buller occupied Dundee on 15 May 1900 and his vanguard reached Newcastle on 18 May, covering 138 miles in nine days; the advance halted to await railway repair.
source: Conan Doyle ch. on Natal advance

description: A squadron of Bethune's Mounted Infantry thrown toward Vryheid to secure the right flank during the advance on Newcastle was ambushed and suffered 66 casualties (nearly half killed).
source: Conan Doyle ch. on Natal advance
note: Exact date uncertain; occurred during Newcastle concentration phase approximately May 18â€“27 1900.

description: Hildyard's Vth Division reached Newcastle on 27 May 1900, completing concentration of the Natal Army after railway repair; Lyttelton and most cavalry had been ordered up from Ladysmith on 23 May.
source: Handbook ch.XIII p.266

description: On 5 June 1900 Buller resumed the advance after an 18-day halt at Newcastle; the western turning route through Botha's Pass was selected to outflank Laing's Nek.
source: Handbook ch.XIII p.267

description: On 6 June Van Wyk's Hill was occupied; artillery was brought up overnight and on 8 June an infantry movement in echelon carried the pass at a cost of only two men killed.
source: Handbook ch.XIII p.267

description: De Wet simultaneously attacked Heilbron Road Station, Rhenoster River Bridge, and Roodeval on 7 June, capturing stores and nearly 500 prisoners; the Roodeval garrison of Derbyshire Militia was overpowered after gallant resistance.
source: Handbook ch.XII p.207â€“208

description: On 11 June 1900 Buller's force stormed Alleman's Nek; the Dorsets of Coke's brigade carried the key conical hill with the bayonet, forcing the Boers to retire overnight.
source: Handbook ch.XIII p.268

description: Following capture of Alleman's Nek, Clery closed up from Ingogo and found the Majubaâ€“Laing's Nekâ€“Pougwana position abandoned; Laing's Nek was occupied on 12 June without opposition.
source: Handbook ch.XIII p.268

description: De Wet recrossed the railway at Lieuw Spruit, took fifty more prisoners and narrowly missed capturing Lord Kitchener who chanced to be passing through; 12 June 1900 is described as one of the most critical days of the war.
source: Handbook ch.XII p.208

description: Buller entered Volksrust, Transvaal, on 13 June 1900; Lyttelton simultaneously received the pro-forma surrender of Wakkerstroom, concluding the eight-month Natal campaign.
source: Handbook ch.XIII p.269

description: Clements and Paget advanced on Bethlehem, occupied on 7 July; the Boers offered only delaying actions before falling back into the Brandwater Basin.
source: Handbook ch.XII p.254

description: De la Rey attacked and captured Uitval's Nek on 11 July as part of a coordinated Boer strategy while Botha engaged Roberts frontally near Pretoria.
source: Conan Doyle ch.26

description: De Wet with Steyn and ~2,600 burghers broke out through unguarded Slabbert's Nek night of 15 Jul 1900, escaping Hunter's encirclement in the Brandwater Basin. ~4,300 men under Prinsloo were left behind and surrendered 30 Jul; De Wet's escape kept the OFS resistance alive.
source: Handbook ch.XII p.255

description: On 7 August 1900 Buller's column of 11,000 men left the Natal railway and marched across the veld, occupying Amersfoort, Ermelo, and Carolina; on 14 August near Carolina it linked up with French's force from Pretoria.
source: Handbook ch.XIII p.270â€“271

description: Hart made a forced march of 36 miles by infantry (54 by cavalry) in 15 hours to surprise and retake Potchefstroom, capturing 80 Boers with little opposition.
source: Conan Doyle ch.31

description: Barton marched south from Krugersdorp through the Gatsrand range in constant skirmishing against commandos of Douthwaite, Liebenberg, and Van der Merwe.
source: Conan Doyle ch.30

description: French led approximately 1,500 mounted men on a cross-country ride from Machadodorp through Carolina to Heidelberg over 14 days; 90 British casualties, 60 Boer prisoners taken.
source: Conan Doyle ch.31

description: Smith-Dorrien advanced south from Belfast to the Komati River; Boer snipers harassed the column throughout and pressed the retirement, but the Canadian rearguard held firm. Boer commanders Prinsloo and Fourie killed, Grobler wounded.
source: Conan Doyle ch.31

description: Viljoen's commando attacked British garrisons at Balmoral (Buffs) and Wilge River (Royal Fusiliers) simultaneously; both attacks were repulsed but the Buffs lost a picket â€” 6 killed, 9 wounded.
source: Conan Doyle ch.31

description: De Wet with 1,500 men rushed the Bloemfonteinâ€“Thabanchu post line at Springhaan's Nek on 16 November, then invested Dewetsdorp; the garrison of 500 held until 23 November when it surrendered, De Wet taking 500 prisoners.
source: Handbook ch.XV p.295â€“296

description: Clements encountered De la Rey with artillery near the Magaliesberg on 24 November and forced him back; returned to Krugersdorp on 26 November with cattle and prisoners.
source: Conan Doyle ch.31

description: Paget attacked Boer forces under Erasmus and Viljoen at Rhenoster Kop; British infantry could not carry the position and suffered ~100 casualties, but Boers retreated overnight due to Lyttelton's flanking demonstration.
source: Conan Doyle ch.31

description: 500 Boers under De la Rey attacked a 150-wagon convoy on the Pretoriaâ€“Rustenburg road; 120 wagons were looted and burned, the escort (West Yorkshires + two guns) holding a kopje with 15 killed and 22 wounded.
source: Conan Doyle ch.31

description: Trapped between flooded rivers after his first Cape Colony raid attempt was blocked, De Wet retreated northward; on 14 December he cut the Bloemfonteinâ€“Ladybrand blockhouse line again at Springhaan's Nek and escaped toward Tafelberg near Senekal.
source: Handbook ch.XV p.296â€“297

description: De Wet detached Kritzinger and Scheepers with orders to watch for an opportunity to break into Cape Colony; they subsequently made their own crossings into the Cape midlands in late December 1900.
source: Handbook ch.XV p.297
note: Exact crossing date uncertain; Cape Colony crossings approximate December 1900.

description: Boers attempted to carry British trenches at Vryheid in a night attack at 2:15 a.m.; repulsed by 2nd Royal Lancaster Regiment, Lancashire Fusiliers, and Royal Garrison Artillery with 14 Boers killed inside the lines.
source: Conan Doyle ch.31

description: Only six days after his defeat at Nooitgedacht, Clements (reinforced by French) returned to the same ground; over 19â€“20 December Boers were shelled out of successive positions and driven from the Magaliesberg.
source: Conan Doyle ch.31

description: Boers stormed the hill post at Helvetia, capturing a 4.7 naval gun and taking 200 Liverpool Regiment prisoners; the garrison lost 50 killed and wounded. Led by Viljoen and Muller.
source: Conan Doyle ch.31

description: De la Rey ambushed squadrons of the Imperial Light Horse in mist; the first volley at 50 yards emptied scores of saddles, killing and wounding approximately 70 before the Boers withdrew.
source: Conan Doyle ch.33

description: Coordinated night attack on Belfast posts at 1 a.m.; Monument Hill post (Royal Irish) and a Shropshire post were carried, but Gordon Highlanders held. British: 60 killed/wounded, 60 prisoners; Boer dead: 24 found inside lines.
source: Conan Doyle ch.33
note: Simultaneous diversionary attacks at Wonderfontein, Wildfontein, Pan, Dalmanutha, and Machadodorp on same night.

description: De Wet assembled over 2,000 burghers on the Doornberg on 25 January 1901 for his second Cape Colony raid; Steyn was present with all available Free State commandants.
source: Handbook ch.XV p.299

commander: French; Smith-Dorrien; Campbell; Alderson; Knox; Allenby; Dartnell
description: Kitchener's great drive with seven columns (~14,000 men) swept the Eastern Transvaal from 27 January; ending 4 April capturing 1,100 men, 11 guns, 4,000 horses, and 1,300 wagons.
source: Conan Doyle ch.33

description: De Wet broke through the Bloemfonteinâ€“Thabanchu post line at Israel's Poort on 30 January, escaping south despite Hamilton being dispatched by rail to reinforce the barrier.
source: Handbook ch.XV p.299â€“300

description: Botha attacked Smith-Dorrien's camp at 3 a.m. in mist with a mounted charge; West Yorkshire veterans repulsed the assault, killing 20 Boers; British suffered 20 killed and 50 wounded.
source: Conan Doyle ch.33

description: De Wet crossed the Orange into Cape Colony at Zand Drift around 10 February 1901, two days ahead of the Knox and Hamilton columns that marched 80 miles along the chord to intercept him.
source: Handbook ch.XV p.301

description: After eighteen days in Cape Colony, De Wet re-entered the Orange River Colony at Botha's Drift on 28 February; Hertzog and Fourie had joined him the night before and the combined force cut through a column under Byng.
source: Handbook ch.XV p.303â€“304

description: 1,500 Boers attacked Lichtenburg garrison of 600 (Paget's Horse and 1st Northumberland Fusiliers) at dawn; garrison was driven to the last trench but held all day; Boers abandoned the attack with 50+ casualties.
source: Conan Doyle ch.33

description: On 11 March, after a trek of more than 800 miles since leaving the Doornberg 44 days earlier, De Wet and Steyn entered Senekal, having dispersed most commandos to their districts.
source: Handbook ch.XV p.304

description: On 24 March Babington's vanguard surprised De la Rey's main force in the open and charged, capturing 2 field guns, 1 pom-pom, 6 maxims, 56 wagons, and 140 prisoners with 54 Boer casualties.
source: Conan Doyle ch.33

description: 500 Boers under Kemp charged through smoke and captured two guns of the 28th R.F.A.; Derbyshires and Scottish Borderers counter-attacked and retook the guns leaving 41 Boers dead. British: 6 officers and 50 men killed, ~120 wounded.
source: Conan Doyle ch.34

description: Viljoen's force attacked 850 of the 5th Victorian Mounted Rifles at night using a veld fire to silhouette the camp; within 5 minutes 20 killed and 40 wounded, survivors surrendered and pom-poms were captured.
source: Conan Doyle ch.34

description: After July 1901 Kitchener extended blockhouse construction from the railways across the open veld of the Orange River Colony, building subsidiary lines at right angles to the railways to fence off Boer movement areas.
source: Handbook ch.XVI p.312â€“313

description: Broadwood surprised the Free State Government at Reitz on 10 July, capturing all OFS government members except Steyn, who escaped; De Wet was absent at the time.
source: Handbook ch.XVI p.311â€“312

description: In early September 1901 Louis Botha assembled approximately 1,000 men in the Ermelo district and marched rapidly toward Natal through the Piet Retief defile and Slangapiesberg, descending on the Blood River area by 17 September.
source: Handbook ch.XIV p.338
note: Exact assembly date uncertain.

description: Major Gough's 300-man force was surrounded and overrun at Scheepers Nek by Botha's commando of over 1,000 men, losing 20 killed, 40 wounded, and over 200 prisoners as well as two guns.
source: Handbook ch.XIV p.338; Conan Doyle ch.36

description: C. Botha attacked Fort Itala at midnight 25 September with several hundred burghers; the hilltop outpost was overrun, but the main garrison of 300 repulsed all attacks until the Boers withdrew, having suffered approximately 100 killed and 200 wounded.
source: Conan Doyle ch.36; Handbook ch.XIV p.338â€“339
note: CONFLICT: Conan Doyle gives midnight Sep 25 start; Handbook gives Sep 26 as date of failure. Both agree on the outcome.

description: Emmett and Grobler attacked Fort Prospect (15 miles east of Fort Itala) with several hundred burghers; Captain Rowley's garrison of Durham Artillery Militia and Dorsets repulsed all attacks with 1 killed and 8 wounded.
source: Conan Doyle ch.36; Handbook ch.XIV p.338â€“339

description: Bruce Hamilton's column arrived at Fort Itala on 28 September in response to the Blood River Poort disaster and the Boer assaults on the fort; Walter Kitchener was simultaneously dispatched to Vryheid to encircle Botha.
source: Conan Doyle ch.36

description: Lyttelton's columns including Bruce Hamilton and Clements converged on the Pondwana Range; Botha was checked by W. Kitchener at a nek between the main heights but escaped eastward three nights later by abandoning his transport.
source: Handbook ch.XIV p.339

description: Boer forces operating in the Zululandâ€“Natal border area intercepted and captured a British convoy from Melmoth, obtaining badly needed supplies as the Natal raid wound down.
source: Conan Doyle ch.36
note: Exact force composition not given.

force: De la Rey and Kemp commando
units: De la Rey and Kemp commando
description: De la Rey attacked Kekewich's camp at Moedwil at dawn; posts on the Selous River were driven in, but a British counter-attack drove De la Rey off by 6 a.m. with 161 British killed and wounded â€” a complete Boer repulse.
source: Conan Doyle ch.36

description: Walter Kitchener's column engaged Botha's rearguard north-east of Vryheid; both sides suffered casualties but Botha shook clear and continued his retirement toward Piet Retief.
source: Conan Doyle ch.36

description: By 11 October 1901 Louis Botha had evaded all British pursuit columns and passed through Piet Retief via the Slangapiesberg defile heading north toward Ermelo; the Natal raid was effectively over.
source: Handbook ch.XIV p.340

force: De la Rey and Kemp commando
units: De la Rey and Kemp commando
description: Kemp charged Von Donop's column flank destroying 8 wagons; De la Rey simultaneously attacked the rearguard, temporarily capturing two guns before Northumberland Fusiliers repelled them. British: 28 killed, 56 wounded; 40 Boers killed.
source: Conan Doyle ch.36

description: Botha with Grobler and Oppermann attacked Benson's column rearguard at Bakenlaagte 30 Oct 1901 in rain and mist. A mounted Boer charge overwhelmed the guns (29 of 32 gunners fell); Benson mortally wounded. British ~235 casualties; 2 guns lost.
source: Conan Doyle ch.36

description: Wessels' commando, using British uniforms and formations to deceive Damant's force, charged the gun position and overwhelmed it; Damant and all his staff were hit with casualties approaching 90 per cent before Rimington drove off the attackers.
source: Conan Doyle ch.36; Handbook ch.XVII p.345â€“346

description: On Christmas morning De Wet's 1,000+ burghers scaled the steep northwest face of Groenkop undetected, overwhelmed the Yeomanry garrison, captured the post with its gun and pom-pom, and escaped before relief arrived; Rundle's HQ was three miles away.
source: Handbook ch.XVII p.347â€“348; Conan Doyle ch.36
note: CONFLICT: Conan Doyle calls it 'Tweefontein, nine miles west of Elands River Bridge'; Handbook calls it 'Groen Kop, half-way between Harrismith and Bethlehem'. Same event, different place-name emphasis.

description: General Ben Viljoen captured by an ambuscade arranged by Major Orr near Lydenburg on 25 January 1902, ending his active command in the eastern Transvaal.
source: Conan Doyle ch.37

description: The first of Kitchener's 'Drive Mark II' â€” 9,000 men drove south from the Frankfortâ€“Kaffir Kop line to the railway; De Wet evaded by breaking through the southern blockhouse line and quietly returned to Elandskop.
source: Handbook ch.XVII p.350â€“351

description: Second OFS 'Drive Mark II': two converging sets of columns (30,000 British troops total) swept northeast ORC; De Wet and Steyn broke through Rawlinson's line at Langverwacht at midnight on 23 February and escaped while remaining commandos were captured.
source: Handbook ch.XVII p.351â€“352

description: Park made a night march and fell upon a Boer laager, capturing 164 prisoners including many Boer officers; the Middelburg commando acting as escort to the Boer government, which escaped.
source: Conan Doyle ch.37

description: De la Rey ambushed Von Donop's convoy escort of 630 men (5th Imperial Yeomanry, Northumberland Fusiliers, 4th R.F.A.) at dawn; Boers outnumbered escort 3:1 and captured two guns and the convoy with 11 officers and 176 men killed or wounded.
source: Conan Doyle ch.38
note: Conan Doyle calls it Jagd Spruit; commonly known as Yzerspruit.

description: Driven from their hiding place near Reitz by a third British drive, De Wet and Steyn crossed the Vaal at Commando Drift on 17 March and joined De la Rey near Wolmaranstad in the Transvaal.
source: Handbook ch.XVII p.352

force: Kekewich, Rawlinson, W. Kitchener, Rochfort columns
units: Kekewich, Rawlinson, W. Kitchener, Rochfort columns
description: Four columns passed through De la Rey's country by night 23 March, spread an 80-mile line and swept back on 24 March, capturing 3 fifteen-pounders, 2 pom-poms, and 175 Boers; columns averaged 70â€“80 miles in 26 hours.
source: Conan Doyle ch.38

description: Cookson pursued a Boer convoy and was attacked by De la Rey's full force; Canadians and 28th MI on the right flank were overwhelmed (18 of 21 Canadians hit), but the British line held until 5:30 p.m. with 200 British casualties and approximately 200 Boer casualties.
source: Conan Doyle ch.38

description: The fifth and final drive in the ORC: Bruce Hamilton swept south from the Eastern Transvaal in co-operation with Elliott for ten days in early May 1902, described as 'the last wriggle in the Orange River Colony.'
source: Handbook ch.XVII p.352â€“353

description: Bethlehem Commando mobilised at Bethlehem on declaration of war, October 1899, forming part of the Boer republican forces.
source: angloboerwar.com unit registry; Handbook of the Boer War

source: angloboerwar.com unit registry; Handbook of the Boer War

source: angloboerwar.com unit registry; Handbook of the Boer War
