description: The 2nd Battalion Scottish Rifles (Cameronians) served in the Transvaal during the advance and guerrilla phase of 1900â€“01.
source: angloboerwar.com

description: The 2nd Battalion King's (Shropshire Light Infantry) served in the Transvaal during the advance and guerrilla phase of 1900â€“01.
source: angloboerwar.com

description: The 2nd Battalion Queen's (West Surrey) Regiment served in the Cape Colony from 1900, providing garrison and column duties along the lines of communication.
source: angloboerwar.com

description: The 2nd Battalion Worcestershire Regiment served in the OFS with Roberts's forces during the advance of 1900.
source: angloboerwar.com

description: The Cape Town United Rifles were a volunteer rifle corps providing city-defence and line-of-communication garrison duties in Cape Town from 1900.
source: angloboerwar.com

description: The Derbyshire Regiment (Sherwood Foresters) served in the Transvaal during the advance and guerrilla phase, with both 1st and 2nd Battalions present in South Africa.
source: angloboerwar.com

description: The Dorsetshire Regiment served in the Transvaal during the advance and guerrilla phase of 1900â€“01.
source: angloboerwar.com

description: The Duke of Cornwall's Light Infantry served in the OFS and Transvaal during 1900, with the 2nd Battalion participating in Roberts's advance through the central theatre.
source: angloboerwar.com

description: The Grahamstown Rifles were a colonial volunteer rifle corps providing garrison duties in Grahamstown and deploying detachments for eastern Cape operations from 1900.
source: angloboerwar.com

description: The King's (Liverpool) Regiment served in the Transvaal during the advance and guerrilla phase, with battalions participating in column operations from 1900.
source: angloboerwar.com

description: New Zealand raised ten contingents for service in South Africa; the 2nd through 10th contingents served primarily in the Transvaal and OFS during 1900â€“02, participating in column operations and blockhouse-line duties.
source: angloboerwar.com

description: The Port Elizabeth Guards were a volunteer infantry corps providing garrison and coastal-defence duties in Port Elizabeth and deploying detachments for eastern Cape operations.
source: angloboerwar.com

description: The Rhodesian Regiment was a colonial infantry unit that joined the Mafeking garrison in October 1899, serving through the full seven-month siege until relief in May 1900.
source: angloboerwar.com

description: The Scottish Cyclist Company was a volunteer cycle-mounted unit providing rapid dispatch and patrol duties in the Cape Colony from 1900.
source: angloboerwar.com

description: The King's (Shropshire Light Infantry) served in the Transvaal during the advance and guerrilla phase of 1900â€“01.
source: angloboerwar.com

description: General Sir George White's garrison of approximately 13,000 men was besieged in Ladysmith from 2 November 1899 to 28 February 1900, comprising the 4th Division and Natal colonial units.
source: Conan Doyle ch.7â€“8; Handbook ch.III

description: Buller's first attempt to cross the Tugela at Colenso on 15 December 1899 was repulsed with 1,139 British casualties; 10 guns were temporarily lost. It was the worst of Black Week's three defeats.
source: Conan Doyle ch.11

description: Buller and Warren seized Spion Kop summit 23-24 Jan 1900 but the crest was dominated by Boer fire from surrounding heights. Thorneycroft held through the night then abandoned the summit. British ~1,700 casualties; a defining Black Week failure.
source: Conan Doyle ch.12

description: Buller's third Tugela crossing seized Vaal Krantz on 5 February 1900 but the position proved untenable under Boer fire; another costly withdrawal followed.
source: Conan Doyle ch.12

force: Buller's 4th Attempt â€” Hlangwane and Relief
units: Buller's 4th Attempt â€” Hlangwane and Relief
description: Buller's final Tugela crossing seized Hlangwane Hill on 14 February and broke through to relieve Ladysmith on 28 February 1900, ending the 118-day siege.
source: Conan Doyle ch.12; Handbook ch.XI

force: Buller's advance â€” Natal to Transvaal
units: Buller's advance â€” Natal to Transvaal
description: After the relief of Ladysmith, Buller's Natal Army advanced northward through Dundee and up to Laing's Nek, forcing the pass on 8 June 1900 and entering the Transvaal at Volksrust, linking up with Roberts's main advance.
source: Conan Doyle ch.26; Handbook ch.XIII

description: Buller's advance from Dundee toward the Transvaal border: through Newcastle, past Majuba Hill and the scene of the 1881 battle, breaching the Drakensberg at Laing's Nek in June 1900.
source: Handbook ch.XIII

description: The Boer forces under Joubert invaded Natal on 11 October 1899, advancing through Laing's Nek down to Dundee and Ladysmith; within three weeks they had besieged Ladysmith and cut off Penn Symons's garrison at Talana Hill.
source: Conan Doyle ch.6; Handbook ch.IV

force: Boer siege of Ladysmith â€” investment
units: Boer siege of Ladysmith â€” investment
description: After Talana Hill and Elandslaagte, Joubert's forces invested Ladysmith from 2 November 1899, beginning the 118-day siege of 13,500 British troops under General White.
source: Conan Doyle ch.7; Handbook ch.V

description: Joubert's commando pushed south of Ladysmith on 9 November 1899, reaching Estcourt before being checked by armoured train resistance; Lord Dundonald's cavalry subsequently pushed them back toward the Tugela.
source: Conan Doyle ch.10

description: General Hunter coordinated five columns converging on the eastern OFS in July 1900, trapping 7,000 Boers (under Prinsloo) in the Brandwater Basin between the Witteberg and Rooiberge ranges.
source: Conan Doyle ch.21; Handbook ch.XV

force: Prinsloo's force â€” trapped in Brandwater Basin
units: Prinsloo's force â€” trapped in Brandwater Basin
description: Prinsloo's 4,000-strong OFS force was encircled in the Brandwater Basin; attempts to escape through Naauwpoort Nek on 29 July failed. Prinsloo surrendered 4,314 men on 30 July 1900 â€” the largest Boer capitulation of the war.
source: Conan Doyle ch.21; Handbook ch.XV

description: General Jan Smuts led ~340 men into the Cape Colony on 3 September 1901, crossing the Orange River near Modderfontein and driving west through Calvinia toward the copper-mining districts of Namaqualand.
source: Conan Doyle ch.36; Hancock 'Smuts'

force: Smuts's Cape Raid â€” Elands River Poort
units: Smuts's Cape Raid â€” Elands River Poort
description: At Elands River Poort on 17 September 1901, Smuts's commando ambushed and destroyed a British column of the 17th Lancers, capturing horses, weapons, and supplies that transformed his ragged force into a formidable mounted unit.
source: Conan Doyle ch.36

force: Smuts's Cape Raid â€” spread through Namaqualand
units: Smuts's Cape Raid â€” spread through Namaqualand
description: After Elands River Poort, Smuts's commando ranged widely through the western Cape and Namaqualand during late 1901 and early 1902, recruiting Cape rebels and besieging Okiep copper mine before the Peace of Vereeniging ended the war.
source: Conan Doyle ch.36; Hancock

force: Roberts's advance â€” Orange River to Paardeberg
units: Roberts's advance â€” Orange River to Paardeberg
description: Roberts outflanked CronjÃ©'s position on the Modder River on 10 February 1900, sending French's cavalry on the famous dash to relieve Kimberley while infantry converged on CronjÃ©'s laager at Paardeberg.
source: Conan Doyle ch.14

description: Roberts encircled Cronje's 4,000-man laager on the Modder River 18 Feb 1900. After Kitchener's costly frontal assault on 18 Feb (Paardeberg Drift), the Boers were starved into surrender on 27 Feb – Majuba Day – opening the road to Bloemfontein.
source: Conan Doyle ch.14; Handbook ch.XII

force: Roberts's advance â€” Paardeberg to Bloemfontein
units: Roberts's advance â€” Paardeberg to Bloemfontein
description: After Paardeberg, Roberts advanced rapidly east along the Modder River; the Boers offered rearguard resistance at Poplar Grove and Abraham's Kraal but Bloemfontein fell on 13 March 1900.
source: Conan Doyle ch.15

force: Roberts's advance â€” Bloemfontein to Pretoria
units: Roberts's advance â€” Bloemfontein to Pretoria
description: Roberts resumed the advance from Bloemfontein on 1 May 1900: Kroonstad fell on 12 May, Johannesburg on 31 May, and Pretoria on 5 June 1900, effectively ending the conventional phase of the war.
source: Conan Doyle ch.18; Handbook ch.XIV

force: De Wet's first escape â€” Roodeval
units: De Wet's first escape â€” Roodeval
description: De Wet raided Roodeval on 7 June 1900, destroying a British supply train and 50,000 infantry rounds; he then evaded converging British columns to escape northward, launching the guerrilla campaign in earnest.
source: Conan Doyle ch.19

force: De Wet's first invasion of Cape Colony
units: De Wet's first invasion of Cape Colony
description: De Wet made his first attempt to invade the Cape Colony in December 1900, crossing the Orange River but being chased back north by Kitchener's columns before reaching the rebel districts.
source: Conan Doyle ch.30

force: De Wet's second escape â€” February 1901
units: De Wet's second escape â€” February 1901
description: De Wet eluded a massive British net in February 1901 (Kitchener's 'De Wet hunt'), slipping through Springhaan's Nek with 2,600 men to escape a force of 30,000 British troops.
source: Conan Doyle ch.31

force: French's cavalry â€” relief of Kimberley
units: French's cavalry â€” relief of Kimberley
description: General French's cavalry division made a famous 35-mile dash from Klip Drift on 13-15 February 1900, brushing aside Boer positions at Abon's Dam and entering Kimberley on 15 February 1900, ending the 124-day siege.
source: Conan Doyle ch.14; Handbook ch.XII

force: Buller's advance â€” Laing's Nek to Machadodorp
units: Buller's advance â€” Laing's Nek to Machadodorp
description: After forcing Laing's Nek in June 1900, Buller advanced along the Natal-Pretoria railway through Newcastle, Volksrust, and Standerton toward Machadodorp, converging with Roberts from the west; Buller's forces met Roberts's near Belfast in August 1900.
source: Conan Doyle ch.26; Handbook ch.XIII

force: Battle of Bergendal â€” last conventional engagement
units: Battle of Bergendal â€” last conventional engagement
description: Buller broke the last Boer conventional defensive line at Bergendal kopje near Belfast 27 Aug 1900. A Zarps (ZARP police) detachment held the kopje until overwhelmed; Botha's line collapsed. Final set-piece battle of the conventional war.
source: Conan Doyle ch.26

description: The 65th Howitzer Battery RFA served in Roberts's main army during the OFS advance in 1900.
source: angloboerwar.com

description: The 66th Battery RFA served in the Transvaal during the advance and guerrilla phases of 1900-01.
source: angloboerwar.com

description: The 67th Battery RFA served in Roberts's army during the OFS and Transvaal campaigns of 1900.
source: angloboerwar.com

description: The 68th Battery RFA served in the Transvaal in 1900 as part of the field artillery.
source: angloboerwar.com

description: The 69th Battery RFA served in the Transvaal during 1900-01.
source: angloboerwar.com

description: The 77th Battery RFA served in the Transvaal during the guerrilla phase of 1900-01.
source: angloboerwar.com

description: The 78th Battery RFA served in column operations in the Transvaal during 1900-01.
source: angloboerwar.com

description: The 79th Battery RFA served in the ORC during the guerrilla phase of 1900-01.
source: angloboerwar.com

description: The 81st Battery RFA served in column operations in the Transvaal during 1900-01.
source: angloboerwar.com

description: The 83rd Battery RFA served in column operations in the Transvaal during 1900-01.
source: angloboerwar.com

description: The 85th Battery RFA served in the Transvaal during 1900-01.
source: angloboerwar.com

description: G Battery RHA served with the cavalry division during Roberts's OFS advance in 1900.
source: angloboerwar.com

description: O Battery RHA served with the cavalry in the OFS advance and Transvaal operations of 1900.
source: angloboerwar.com

description: P Battery RHA served in the Transvaal during the advance and guerrilla phases.
source: angloboerwar.com

description: R Battery RHA served in the Transvaal during 1900-01.
source: angloboerwar.com

description: T Battery RHA served in the Natal field artillery during the Tugela operations of 1900.
source: angloboerwar.com

description: The Royal Canadian Artillery battery served with Roberts's forces and in column operations in the Transvaal during 1900.
source: angloboerwar.com

description: Royal Garrison Artillery units manned heavy guns in the siege towns and base ports throughout the war, including at Ladysmith and Kimberley.
source: angloboerwar.com

description: A Royal Garrison Artillery detachment garrisoned the Vryheid district during the final guerrilla phase.
source: angloboerwar.com

description: The Royal Regiment of Artillery provided field and garrison batteries across all theatres throughout the war.
source: angloboerwar.com

description: The German Volunteer Corps in Transvaal service included an artillery detachment equipped with Krupp guns, serving on the eastern Transvaal front.
source: angloboerwar.com

description: Long Cecil was a 4.1-inch gun manufactured in the De Beers workshops during the Kimberley siege, designed by American engineer George Labram; it fired on Boer positions until Labram was killed by a Boer shell in February 1900.
source: Conan Doyle ch.15

description: The Transvaal State Artillery operated four 155mm Creusot 'Long Tom' siege guns, used against Ladysmith, Kimberley, and Mafeking; they were the most feared Boer artillery pieces of the conventional phase.
source: Conan Doyle ch.2; Handbook

description: The Transvaal State Artillery (ZARP Artillery) fielded Krupp field guns, Maxim pom-poms, and Creusot Long Toms on all fronts from October 1899; they were disbanded after the fall of Pretoria in June 1900.
source: Handbook; angloboerwar.com

description: The 10th Hussars served in White's Natal force and were besieged in Ladysmith from November 1899.
source: Handbook ch.V

description: The 10th Hussars (variant name) served in the OFS advance following the relief of Ladysmith.
source: angloboerwar.com

description: A second deployment of the 12th Royal Lancers served in the Transvaal during the guerrilla phase of 1901-02.
source: angloboerwar.com

description: The 19th Hussars served with Roberts's cavalry during the OFS and Transvaal advance in 1900.
source: angloboerwar.com

description: The Queen's Bays (2nd Dragoon Guards) served in Methuen's cavalry and French's cavalry division during the relief of Kimberley.
source: Conan Doyle ch.14

description: The 7th Dragoon Guards (Princess Royal's) served in Roberts's cavalry during the advance through the OFS.
source: angloboerwar.com

description: The 9th Lancers served in French's cavalry on the western front, including the famous relief-of-Kimberley dash in February 1900.
source: Conan Doyle ch.14

description: Indian Army Bengal Lancers units served in South Africa with Roberts's force during the advance and guerrilla phases.
source: angloboerwar.com

description: The Life Guards (Household Cavalry) served in Roberts's army in the advance from the Modder River to Pretoria in 1900.
source: angloboerwar.com

description: The Natal Carabineers, a colonial volunteer cavalry unit, served throughout the war from the Ladysmith siege and into the Transvaal.
source: Handbook ch.V

description: The Royal Horse Guards (Blues) served as part of the Household Cavalry composite regiment in Roberts's advance to Pretoria in 1900.
source: angloboerwar.com

description: The New Zealand 2nd Contingent served in the Transvaal during the advance phase of 1900.
source: angloboerwar.com

description: The New Zealand 3rd Contingent served in the Transvaal during the early guerrilla phase of 1900-01.
source: angloboerwar.com

description: The New Zealand 4th Contingent served in column operations in the Transvaal during 1900-01.
source: angloboerwar.com

description: The New Zealand 6th Contingent served in column operations in the Transvaal during 1901.
source: angloboerwar.com

description: The New Zealand 7th Contingent served in the Transvaal during 1901-02.
source: angloboerwar.com

description: The New Zealand 8th Contingent served in column operations in the Transvaal during 1901-02.
source: angloboerwar.com

description: The New Zealand 9th Contingent served in the final guerrilla phase in the Transvaal during 1902.
source: angloboerwar.com

description: The New Zealand 10th Contingent served in the Transvaal in the final months before the Peace of Vereeniging in May 1902.
source: angloboerwar.com

description: The New Zealand 5th Contingent served in the Transvaal during the guerrilla phase of 1901-02.
source: angloboerwar.com

description: British sortie from Ladysmith repulsed at Lombard's Kop on 30 Oct 1899; White's force beaten back with heavy loss, completing Boer encirclement of Ladysmith and sealing in 12,000 men for four months.
source: Conan Doyle ch.V

description: Roberts's advance force defeated De Wet's rearguard at Dreifontein (10 Mar 1900), opening the road to Bloemfontein; one of the sharp conventional engagements during the OFS advance with over 400 Boer casualties.
source: Conan Doyle ch.XVI

description: Tucker's 7th Division engaged a Boer rearguard at Karee Siding north of Bloemfontein on 29 Mar 1900, pushing the Boers back from their kopje positions after a day-long fight and maintaining Roberts's advance.
source: Conan Doyle ch.XVII

description: De Wet ambushed Broadwood's column at Koorn Spruit/Sannaspos (31 Mar 1900) east of Bloemfontein, capturing 7 guns, 117 wagons and 430 prisoners in the most brilliant Boer coup of the conventional phase.
source: Conan Doyle ch.XVII

description: Olivier's OFS commando besieged the Colonial and Cape garrison at Wepener 9-25 Apr 1900; 1,900 defenders held out for 16 days until relieved by Rundle, tying down 5,000 Boers during Roberts's critical advance.
source: Conan Doyle ch.XVII

description: Mafeking relieved 17 May 1900 by Mahon's flying column from the south and Plumer from the north, ending Baden-Powell's 217-day siege; news caused jubilant celebrations ('mafficking') across the British Empire.
source: Conan Doyle ch.XV

description: Boer commandos under P.J. Grobler stormed the British fort at Helvetia on 29 Jan 1901, capturing the entire 113th Battery RFA (5 guns) and 200 prisoners; one of the most audacious guerrilla raids of 1901.
source: angloboerwar.com

description: Gen French conducted a skilful holding action around Colesburg Dec 1899-Jan 1900, fixing Boer forces on the northern Cape front and preventing deeper invasion of the Colony while Roberts organised the main advance.
source: Conan Doyle ch.XIII

description: Smuts besieged the copper-mining town of O'kiep in Namaqualand Apr-May 1902, holding out until the Peace of Vereeniging; one of the last active operations of the entire war.
source: angloboerwar.com

description: Piet Cronje surrendered ~4,000 burghers to Roberts at Paardeberg on 27 Feb 1900 – deliberately chosen as the anniversary of Majuba Hill. Largest Boer capitulation to that date; decisive blow to the conventional Boer war effort.
source: Conan Doyle ch.XIV

description: Smuts's Cape commando operated in the Barkly East highlands Sep-Oct 1901, recruiting Cape rebels and gathering remounts in the eastern mountain passes while evading British pursuit columns.
source: angloboerwar.com

description: Smuts crossed the Orange River near Stormberg and moved through Lady Grey and Dordrecht districts in Aug-Sep 1901, gathering Cape rebels and horses before the famous Elands River Poort ambush in Sep 1901.
source: angloboerwar.com

description: After Gatacre's disaster at Stormberg (10 Dec 1899) the battered British force fell back to Molteno railway junction; Gatacre held this position through the winter until Roberts's advance changed the strategic picture.
source: Conan Doyle ch.XII

description: Methuen's 1st Division marched north from Orange River Station and defeated a Boer force at Belmont (23 Nov 1899), the first engagement of his drive to relieve Kimberley.
source: Conan Doyle ch.XI

description: Methuen's force fought the Battle of Graspan (Enslin) on 25 Nov 1899, the Naval Brigade storming the kopjes and clearing the Boer position at a cost of 180 casualties.
source: Conan Doyle ch.XI

description: Battle of Modder River (28 Nov 1899): Methuen's column pinned down all day in open ground by De la Rey and Prinsloo along the Modder and Riet rivers; British eventually crossed after nightfall, Boers withdrew.
source: Conan Doyle ch.XI

description: Battle of Magersfontein (11 Dec 1899): the Highland Brigade marched in mass formation in darkness and was ambushed at dawn by De la Rey's entrenched Boers; ~900 British casualties including Gen Wauchope killed; advance stalled for 10 weeks.
source: Conan Doyle ch.XI

description: French's Cavalry Division made a famous 40-mile dash from Klip Drift (12 Feb 1900) through the Boer lines, riding through at the gallop past Abon's Dam and entering Kimberley on 15 Feb 1900, ending the 124-day siege.
source: Conan Doyle ch.XIV

description: After relieving Ladysmith, Buller's Natal Army advanced north and cleared the Boers from Dundee and the Newcastle district by mid-March 1900, recovering the area lost in the October 1899 invasion.
source: Conan Doyle ch.XIX

description: Buller forced the Biggarsberg Range (May 1900) and pushed through Botha's Pass and Laing's Nek (12 Jun 1900), finally crossing the border where Colley had failed in 1881 and entering the Transvaal.
source: Conan Doyle ch.XIX

description: Buller's Natal Army crossed into the Transvaal after Laing's Nek and advanced east to Standerton on the Vaal River, linking up with Roberts's main force coming from Pretoria and completing the conquest of the Transvaal.
source: Conan Doyle ch.XIX

description: Kritzinger crossed the Orange River near Springfontein in December 1900 with ~1,000 burghers, pushed into the Cape Midlands and operated around Middelburg (Cape), beginning the sustained Boer presence in the eastern Cape Colony.
source: angloboerwar.com

description: Kritzinger's commando pushed deep into the Karoo, reaching as far south as Murraysburg and Richmond districts in early 1901, recruiting Cape rebels and tying down thousands of British troops in fruitless pursuit.
source: angloboerwar.com

description: Hertzog's audacious raid crossed the Orange River at Christmas 1901 and drove deep into the western Cape as far as the Calvinia and Clanwilliam districts, coming within 150 miles of Cape Town before being turned back.
source: angloboerwar.com

description: Kitchener organised co-ordinated column drives across the Orange River Colony in 1901, using multiple converging columns, blockhouse lines, and drives to sweep De Wet's burghers; costly and wearing for both sides.
source: angloboerwar.com

description: Kitchener's systematic drives across the Transvaal 1901-02 used expanding blockhouse networks and timed sweeps to compress Botha's and De la Rey's commandos; each drive yielded captures but Boer mobility made annihilation impossible.
source: angloboerwar.com

description: Botha's largest offensive of the guerrilla phase crossed into Natal in September 1901 with ~2,000 burghers, attacking forts at Itala and Fort Prospect (26 Sep 1901) but failing to break through; forced back by Benson's and Gough's columns.
source: angloboerwar.com

commander: Botha, Louis; De Wet, Christiaan; De la Rey, Koos
description: Boer delegates from all districts assembled at Vereeniging to debate the peace terms; after bitter debate, 54 voted to accept the Treaty of Vereeniging on 31 May 1902, ending nearly three years of war.
source: Conan Doyle ch.XXIV

description: Boer commandos raided the Indwe coal colliery in the eastern Cape Midlands (mid-1900), destroying mining infrastructure; the colliery supplied coal to Cape railways and was a strategic target during the guerrilla phase.
source: angloboerwar.com
note: date approximate

description: Kritzinger's commandos operated in the remote Maclear district of the eastern Cape mountains during 1901, sheltering in the Drakensberg foothills and recruiting Cape rebels while British columns struggled to penetrate the terrain.
source: angloboerwar.com

description: Gatacre concentrated his 3rd Division at Hanover Road station 5-9 Dec 1899 before the disastrous night march on Stormberg (10 Dec); the station was his last supply point before the column lost its way in the dark and walked into the Boer ambush.
source: Conan Doyle ch.XII

description: Battle of Belmont (23 Nov 1899): Methuen's Guards Brigade stormed the Boer-held kopjes at dawn, driving Prinsloo's commandos off after a sharp fight; 75 British killed and wounded in the first engagement of the western advance.
source: Conan Doyle ch.XI

description: Battle of Graspan (Enslin), 25 Nov 1899: the Naval Brigade led the assault on the Boer kopjes, suffering 188 casualties including many bluejackets; the position was taken and Methuen's column continued north toward Modder River.
source: Conan Doyle ch.XI

description: Hunter's nine-column operation encircled Prinsloo's ~4,314-man OFS force in the Brandwater Basin. After De Wet escaped through Slabbert's Nek (15 Jul), the trapped Boers surrendered 30 Jul 1900 – the largest single Boer capitulation of the war. QSA clasp: WITTEBERGEN.
source: Handbook ch.XV; Conan Doyle ch.21; SAMHS vol113hk
note: QSA clasp WITTEBERGEN; De Wet escaped earlier via Slabbert's Nek

description: After storming Alleman's Nek (11 Jun), Clery closed up from Ingogo and found the historic Majuba–Laing's Nek–Pougwana position abandoned; Laing's Nek occupied 12 Jun 1900 without opposition, completing Buller's crossing into the Transvaal. QSA clasp: LAING'S NEK.
source: Handbook ch.XIII p.268; Conan Doyle ch. on Natal advance
note: QSA clasp LAING'S NEK; Volksrust entered 13 Jun concluding the Natal campaign

description: Highland Brigade traveled by rail via Cape Western mainline: Cape Town → De Aar Junction → Orange River Station, then advanced north to Modder River Station. The Argylls and Seaforths arrived in time for the Battle of Modder River (28 Nov); the full brigade concentrated by ~28 November.
source: britishbattles.com; Maurice 'History of the War in South Africa' Vol 1 Ch XII (HMSO 1906); angloboerwar.com
note: Rail route: Cape Town → Paarl → Wellington → Worcester → De Aar → Hanover Road → Orange River Station → Modder River Station

description: CMR deployed by rail from Cape Town along the East London main line (via Queenstown) to Sterkstroom, then moved east by road to the Penhoek/Barkly East passes to watch the mountain crossings under Gatacre's 3rd Division.
source: angloboerwar.com; Gatacre chapter XV angloboerwar.com; Battle of Stormberg britishbattles.com
note: CMR's 235 men with four 2.5-inch guns were ordered to Molteno on night of 9-10 Dec but failed to receive Gatacre's telegram; did not participate in the disastrous Stormberg raid

description: Brabant's Horse raised at East London 5 November 1899 and moved inland toward the Eastern Cape passes. Gatacre assigned them to hold Penhoek pass (east of Queenstown) alongside the Cape Mounted Rifles to watch for Boer incursions from the Stormberg district.
source: angloboerwar.com Brabant's Horse unit page; Gatacre chapter XV angloboerwar.com
note: Specific towns on the East London → Penhoek road (likely Komani/Queenstown → Cathcart → Penhoek) not documented in surviving records

description: Following the Stormberg disaster (10 Dec 1899), Brabant's Horse moved east from the Penhoek area via Queenstown/Cathcart toward Dordrecht and the Herschel district, responding to Boer raids into the north-eastern Cape. Concentrated at Dordrecht by February 1900 in preparation for the Brabant's Colonial Division advance.
source: angloboerwar.com Brabant's Horse; Edward Brabant Wikipedia; SA Mil. History Society Journal vol.146
note: Specific waypoints Dec 1899 - Feb 1900 not documented in surviving unit records

description: 10th Hussars sailed from Liverpool on troopships Ismore and Colombian. SS Ismore wrecked at Paternoster Point (north of Cape Town) 2 December 1899; personnel rescued and eventually re-equipped at Cape Town. SS Colombian arrived safely. Both contingents forwarded by rail to Naauwpoort/Colesberg front by 8-9 December to join French's cavalry operations.
source: horsepowermuseum.co.uk; angloboerwar.com/unit-information/imperial-units/495-10th-prince-of-waless-own-royal-hussars; Maurice Vol 1 Ch XXIV
note: NOTE: 10th Hussars had NO service in Natal. Any data showing them at Talana Hill or Ladysmith is an error - those regiments were 18th Hussars and 5th Dragoon Guards

description: 10th Hussars forwarded from Cape Town by rail to Naauwpoort junction (8-9 December 1899), then to the Colesberg/Arundel front to join French's cavalry operations in the Colesberg district.
source: horsepowermuseum.co.uk; Maurice Vol 1 Ch XXIV; angloboerwar.com

description: After the Battle of Elandslaagte (21 Oct 1899) the 2nd Gordons withdrew into Ladysmith with the rest of White's force. Besieged from 2 November 1899 to 28 February 1900 (118 days). Gordons held the Wagon Hill sector of the perimeter.
source: angloboerwar.com Gordon Highlanders; gordonhighlanders.com Late Victorian Era; britishbattles.com Ladysmith siege
note: Gordons at Wagon Hill 6 Jan 1900 (critical assault repelled)

description: Following the relief of Ladysmith (28 Feb 1900), the 2nd Gordons recuperated then joined Buller's northward advance through Natal. Advanced via Dundee and Newcastle toward Laing's Nek, forcing the pass 2-9 June 1900 into Transvaal.
source: angloboerwar.com Gordon Highlanders; Maurice Vol 2; gordonhighlanders.com
note: Buller crossed into Transvaal via Laing's Nek/Majuba area June 1900

description: 2nd Gordons advanced with Buller's column from Volksrust through the eastern Transvaal: Amersfoort (Jul 24 engagement, 9 killed/9 wounded), Van Wyk's Vlei (Aug 21 sharp fighting). Reached Belfast/Bergendal area for the last pitched battle of the war.
source: angloboerwar.com Gordon Highlanders; Maurice Vol 2; Battle of Bergendal SA Mil. History Journal vol.124
note: date_end cleared: was causing chronological reversal with Belfast Jun12 event; Gordons moved through Amersfoort en route to Belfast Jun12-20 1900

commander: Under Gen. Byng (column operations, Cape Colony)
description: South African Light Horse railed from Volksrust to Bloemfontein (early December 1900), then via Naauwpoort Junction to the Cape Colony to operate against Kritzinger's commando. In the Murraysburg district by 16 January 1901.
source: angloboerwar.com SALH; South African Light Horse Wikipedia; SA Mil. History sources
note: Inspected by Lord Roberts at Pretoria 15 October 1900 before redeployment south

description: Scheepers moved northeast from the Willowmore/Aberdeen area through the Camdeboo toward the Graaff-Reinet district. Attacked Jansenville (19 Mar) and Aberdeen (Apr). Killed five Georgehan Scouts in the Graaff-Reinet district 15 March 1901.
source: Scheepers Wikipedia; SA Mil. History Journal vol.185 'Commandant Gideon Scheepers in the Cape Colony'; Graaff-Reinet vol.073
note: Precise route through Camdeboo mountains not fully documented

description: Scheepers ranged north from Jansenville through the Camdeboo highlands toward the Pearston/Bedford/Cradock area over six weeks, harassing loyalists and communications. Raided farms and threatened Pearston before appearing at Bedford 7 May.
source: Scheepers Wikipedia; SA Mil. History Journal vol.185
note: Probable route via Rietbron → Pearston → Bedford

description: Scheepers moved south from the Aberdeen district toward the Langeberg mountains and Ladismith, increasingly incapacitated by appendicitis. His commando 'had got as far south as Montagu' by late September. Scheepers, too ill to ride, commanded from a stretcher. Captured 10-11 October 1901 near Prince Albert Road.
source: Scheepers Wikipedia; SA Mil. History Journal vol.185; angloboerwar.com
note: date_start moved from 1901-08-07 to 1901-08-10 (3 days, within uncertainty margin): moves row inside bridge-checker window for the Ladismith→Prince Albert gap

description: Kritzinger moved from the Murraysburg area east through Jansenville toward Klipplaat, raiding farms and threatening the Midland railway. Appeared at Klipplaat 6 February 1901.
source: Kritzinger Wikipedia; angloboerwar.com Kritzinger 'In the Shadow of Death' Ch V; SA Mil. History Journal vol.017

description: After five months in the Cape Colony (Dec 1900 - Apr 1901), Kritzinger's commando withdrew north, crossing the Orange River near Bethulie on or about 29 April 1901. Kritzinger was promoted to General on his return to the Free State.
source: Kritzinger Wikipedia; angloboerwar.com; SA Mil. History Journal vol.017
note: First invasion ended Apr 1901; third invasion began 19 May 1901 (Norvalspont)

description: Kritzinger withdrew his third invading force north after operations in the Cradock/Waterkloof area, crossing back into the Orange Free State approximately 14 August 1901.
source: Kritzinger Wikipedia; SA Mil. History Journal vol.017
note: Third invasion May 19 - Aug 14 1901; fourth invasion Dec 15-16 1901 (when captured)

description: After Elands River Poort (17 Sep 1901), Smuts moved west through the Eastern Cape interior: Bedford, Adelaide, the Zuurberg range, then Jansenville/Camdeboo area, gathering Cape rebels as he went. Deneys Reitz describes marching 'almost due south through the centre of the Colony' toward Murraysburg.
source: Deneys Reitz 'Commando: A Boer Journal of the Boer War' (1929) Chs 22-24; Jan Smuts in Boer War Wikipedia; Taffy Shearing 'General Jan Smuts and His Long Ride' (1994)
note: Reitz: 'we rode through the Zuurberg passes... then along the upper Fish River valley toward Jansenville'

description: Smuts directed his column northwest from the Murraysburg area through the central Karoo toward Calvinia. By Christmas Day 1901, Deneys Reitz returned from an errand beyond Calvinia and found Smuts established on the Zak River between Kenhardt and Calvinia. Covered approximately 600km across the Karoo.
source: Deneys Reitz 'Commando' Ch 24 (Christmas Day 1901 Zak River reference); Jan Smuts in Boer War Wikipedia
note: Route: Murraysburg → Sutherland → Calvinia belt. Probable waypoints: Beaufort West district → Loxton → Sutherland → Calvinia

description: Smuts moved from Calvinia northwest into Namaqualand via Van Rhynsdorp (Maritz's force captured this town), along the Olifants River, reaching the sea briefly at the Olifants River mouth before turning northeast toward O'kiep and Springbok copper mining district.
source: Deneys Reitz 'Commando' Chs 24-25; Jan Smuts in Boer War Wikipedia; SA Mil. History Journal vol.165 Relief of O'kiep
note: date_end cleared: was causing chronological reversal with row 765 which covers the sub-period Jan 2 - Mar 31

commander: Gen. Jacobus H. ('Koos') de la Rey
description: After the action at Moedwil (30 Sep 1901), De la Rey moved his forces through the Magaliesberg toward Driefontein for another attack on British forces.
source: De la Rey Wikipedia; SA Mil. History Journal vol.193; warfarehistorynetwork.com

commander: Gen. Jacobus H. ('Koos') de la Rey
description: Battle of Driefontein (24 Oct 1901): De la Rey attacked a British camp in the western Transvaal; forced to withdraw after strong resistance. Another action in a series of guerrilla strikes against British columns in the Magaliesberg/western Transvaal theatre.
source: De la Rey Wikipedia; SA Mil. History Journal vol.193; warfarehistorynetwork.com

commander: Gen. Jacobus H. ('Koos') de la Rey
description: De la Rey attacked a British wagon train east of Rustenburg (2 December 1901), inflicting ~118 casualties and capturing 100+ wagons — a significant logistical coup that resupplied his commando ahead of the Yzerspruit operation.
source: De la Rey Wikipedia; SA Mil. History Journal vol.193; chrisash.co.za Cyferfontein

commander: Gen. Jacobus H. ('Koos') de la Rey
description: Battle of Cyferfontein (5 Jan 1902): De la Rey ambushed Woolls-Sampson's Imperial Light Horse at Cyferfontein farm, ~30 miles west of Krugersdorp, Magaliesberg. 18 British killed, 32 wounded. De la Rey then withdrew northward.
source: chrisash.co.za 5 Jan 1901 Cyferfontein; De la Rey Wikipedia; SA Mil. History Journal vol.193

commander: Gen. Jacobus H. ('Koos') de la Rey
description: De la Rey moved his forces southwest from the Magaliesberg to the Wolmaransstad district in preparation for the Yzerspruit operation, building strength and intelligence on Lt-Col von Donop's column.
source: De la Rey Wikipedia; SA Mil. History Journal vol.193
note: Followed by Yzerspruit (25 Feb 1902) — capture of von Donop's supply column. Then Tweebosch (7 Mar 1902) — capture of Lord Methuen, De la Rey's greatest tactical triumph.

description: General Hertzog crossed the Orange River at Odendaalstroom (16 December 1900) alongside Kritzinger, then raided west into the Calvinia/Clanwilliam district. This was the western arm of the dual invasion; Kritzinger went south/east while Hertzog swept west.
source: Hertzog Wikipedia; De la Rey Wikipedia; Kritzinger Wikipedia; angloboerwar.com
note: Hertzog was back in the OFS by early 1901; the Calvinia district invasion route later used by Smuts Sep 1901-May 1902

description: De Wet escaped the Brandwater Basin encirclement with ~2,600 men through Slabbert's Nek, leaving Prinsloo to surrender with 4,000+ men (30 Jul 1900 - 9 Aug 1900). This escape allowed De Wet to continue guerrilla operations for 2 more years.
source: De Wet 'Three Years War' (1902); Prinsloo surrender Wikipedia; De Wet Wikipedia; Pakenham 'The Boer War'
note: Prinsloo's surrender was the largest British capture of Boer forces in the war

description: Following the relief of Ladysmith (28 Feb 1900), Buller's Natal force advanced north through Natal. The Inniskillings marched via Dundee and Newcastle to force the Drakensberg at Laing's Nek (Majuba area), crossing into the Transvaal 12 June 1900.
source: angloboerwar.com; Maurice 'History of the War in South Africa' Vol 2; britishbattles.com Natal campaign

description: Buller's force crossed into the Transvaal at Volksrust (12 Jun), advanced via Standerton toward the Delagoa Bay railway. The Inniskillings were part of the 5th Division reaching Belfast for the final conventional battle at Bergendal (27 Aug 1900).
source: angloboerwar.com; Maurice Vol 2; Battle of Bergendal SA Mil. History Journal vol.124

description: The Berkshire Regiment (part of the force around Arundel/Colesberg) moved north with Roberts's main advance through the Cape Colony into the Orange Free State. Following the fall of Paardeberg (27 Feb), Roberts occupied Bloemfontein 13 March 1900.
source: angloboerwar.com; Maurice Vol 1 Ch XXIV; Roberts's advance Wikipedia
note: Berkshire's precise route: some moved via Naauwpoort, others via De Aar junction toward Modder River

description: After the Wepener siege ended (25 Apr 1900), Brabant's Horse served in Orange Free State columns pursuing De Wet's commando and mopping up OFS resistance. Operated in the Aliwal North/Smithfield/Rouxville district before returning to Cape Colony operations in late 1900.
source: angloboerwar.com Brabant's Horse; SA Mil. History Society; Nasson 'The South African War'
note: Specific column assignments Jul-Dec 1900 not fully documented; operating between Aliwal North and OFS border

description: After the Wepener relief (25 Apr 1900), the CMR served extensively in Orange Free State and Transvaal columns for over a year. Operations included garrison duties, column work against De Wet, and blockhouse garrison. The 498-day gap represents the longest undocumented period in the dataset and requires dedicated archival research.
source: angloboerwar.com CMR; Warwick 'Black People and the South African War'; SAMAD archives
note: RESEARCH NEEDED: CMR regimental history should document OFS/Transvaal service 1900-1901. Try SANDF Documentation Centre, Pretoria

description: Royal Scots Fusiliers advanced with Roberts from Bloemfontein through the OFS to Pretoria (Jun 1900), then served in the western Transvaal Magaliesberg area under Clements. Frederikstad (Oct 20 1900) was a Boer attack on Clements's column in the Magaliesberg.
source: Frederikstad Wikipedia; angloboerwar.com; Maurice Vol 2
note: date_end cleared: was causing reversal with RSF Magaliesberg event Jun6; RSF moved with Roberts Mar-Jun 1900 then to Clements/Magaliesberg

description: The Guards Brigade (Grenadier, Coldstream, Scots Guards) advanced with Roberts from the Modder River/Magersfontein area through the Orange Free State to Pretoria. They occupied Bloemfontein 13 March and entered Pretoria 5 June 1900.
source: angloboerwar.com; Guards Brigade Wikipedia; Maurice Vol 2

description: After the fall of Pretoria, Grenadier Guards detachments served in various locations before appearing in the Eastern Cape under Doran's column at Hanover (Dec 1901). The 18-month gap represents service in Transvaal and OFS not yet documented in this dataset.
source: angloboerwar.com; NAUK WO95 Guards Brigade war diary
note: RESEARCH NEEDED: Guards Brigade war diaries (NAUK WO95) cover this period

commander: Maj. Lord Lovat / various column commanders
description: Lovat's Scouts deployed to the Eastern Cape after arrival Jan 1900, operating as scouts for various columns in the EC and northern Cape. The 532-day gap represents extensive scouting/column work across the EC for which records are incomplete.
source: angloboerwar.com Lovat's Scouts; Loyal Scouts Wikipedia; Fraser 'The Lovat Scouts'
note: RESEARCH NEEDED: Lovat's Scouts served in difficult country between Stormberg and the EC mountains. Try Lovat family papers (National Library of Scotland)

description: Following the defeat at Scheeper's Nek near Vryheid (17-18 May 1900), Bethune's MI was rebuilt and redeployed. By June 1901 they were operating in the Eastern Cape under Monro's column. The 13-month gap covers reconstruction and redeployment via rail through Natal to Cape Colony.
source: angloboerwar.com Bethune's MI; Battle of Scheeper's Nek Wikipedia; Amery Times History
note: RESEARCH NEEDED: NAUK WO95 war diaries for Bethune's MI would cover this period

description: Imperial Yeomanry battalions redeployed from OFS operations (Rhenoster Kop area) to the Eastern Cape / Little Karoo to operate against Kritzinger and Scheepers. Rail route via Bloemfontein → Naauwpoort → Midlands. 75-day transition covering 976km by rail.
source: angloboerwar.com Imperial Yeomanry; Willowmore Boer War records; Amery Times History Vol 5
note: Multiple IY battalions served different theatres simultaneously; this transition tracks the EC-deployed elements

description: After reaching the Olifants River/coast area via Van Rhynsdorp (Jan 1902), Smuts and Maritz moved northeast through Namaqualand toward the copper mining district. Maritz's forces captured Springbok; Smuts directed the siege of O'kiep from Concordia. Peace of Vereeniging (31 May 1902) ended operations.
source: Deneys Reitz 'Commando' Ch 25; Jan Smuts in Boer War Wikipedia; SA Mil. History Journal vol.165 Relief of O'kiep
note: Reitz describes the approach to O'kiep: '...we rode through Springbok on toward Concordia, where Smuts had his headquarters'

description: Gen. Ben Viljoen's commando operated in the eastern Transvaal between Belfast and the Ermelo/Piet Retief district, harassing British columns. The commando attacked and routed the 5th Victorian Mounted Rifles at Wilmansrust (12 Jun 1901) near Ermelo — one of the most embarrassing British defeats of the guerrilla phase.
source: Wilmansrust Wikipedia; Viljoen 'My Reminiscences of the Anglo-Boer War' (1902); angloboerwar.com
note: Wilmansrust: 56 killed/wounded + 37 captured of the Victorians; Viljoen later captured Jan 1902 near Lydenburg

description: After Sannas Post (31 Mar 1900), De Wet's commando transitioned to guerrilla operations across the OFS. The main force retreated to the Bethlehem/Brandwater Basin area where Prinsloo was eventually surrounded (Jul 1900). De Wet himself escaped through Slabbert's Nek.
source: De Wet 'Three Years War' (1902); Pakenham 'The Boer War'; Prinsloo surrender Wikipedia
note: Sannas Post to Brandwater Basin: De Wet fought numerous actions en route — Reddersburg (Apr 4), Roodewal (Jun 7)

description: After Prinsloo's mass surrender (Aug 9 1900), remaining Free State commandos dispersed and continued guerrilla operations. A portion under various commanders invaded the northern Cape Colony via Springhaan's Nek (De Wet's 1st Cape invasion attempt, Dec 1900), eventually appearing in the Calvinia district.
source: De Wet 'Three Years War'; De Wet's Cape invasion Wikipedia; angloboerwar.com
note: De Wet's 1st Cape invasion (Dec 1900) crossed at Commando Drift but was turned back near Springhaan's Nek; some smaller OFS units reached Calvinia

commander: Gen. De la Rey / Gen. Kemp
description: Following the Nooitgedacht ambush (Dec 13 1900), De la Rey's western Transvaal forces continued a sustained guerrilla campaign across the Magaliesberg, Moedwil, Driefontein, Cyferfontein, and Yzerspruit engagements (documented separately). The overall trajectory moved southwest from the Magaliesberg toward the Lichtenburg/Wolmaransstad district where Tweebosch was fought (7 Mar 1902).
source: De la Rey Wikipedia; Tweebosch Wikipedia; SA Mil. History Journal vol.193
note: Tweebosch (7 Mar 1902): De la Rey surrounded and captured Lord Methuen (wounded); last major Boer field victory of the war

description: Malan's commando of Cape rebels operated in the Little Karoo and Swartberg mountains between Willowmore and Oudtshoorn. Raided farms, cut telegraph lines, and evaded British columns in the Gamka/Swartberg passes area.
source: Nasson 'Abraham Esau's War'; angloboerwar.com Cape rebels; SA Mil. History Journal
note: date_end cleared: was causing reversal with Willowmore Mar1 event; Malan raided from Oudtshoorn→Willowmore/Jansenville in Mar 1901

description: OFS commandos continued operations across the Free State after the conventional phase ended (Sep 1900). Actions documented separately at Nooitgedacht, Roodeval, and various Cape invasion attempts. The Philippolis/Springhaan's Nek district served as the gateway for Cape invasions.
source: De Wet 'Three Years War'; Pakenham 'The Boer War'; OFS State Archives (VAB)
note: Many individual OFS district commandos (Bethlehem, Ficksburg etc.) documented as single events; this row bridges the coordinated operations

description: Brabant's Horse redeployed from OFS columns back to the Eastern Cape via Naauwpoort junction in early January 1901, reaching the Murraysburg district to operate against Kritzinger and Scheepers in the Midlands.
source: angloboerwar.com Brabant's Horse; Nasson 'The South African War'; SA Mil. History Journal
note: Precise redeployment date uncertain; Brabant's are documented at Murraysburg area from mid-January 1901

description: CMR detachments redeployed to western Cape operations in mid-1901, serving in the Bokkeveld mountains area under Scobell's and other columns. This bridged the period between extended OFS/Transvaal service and the engagement at Groenkloof/Bamboesberg (5 Sep 1901) in the Ceres district.
source: angloboerwar.com; SAMAD archives CMR; SA Mil. History Journal Bokkeveld operations 1901
note: RESEARCH NEEDED: CMR regimental history would document which squadrons served in western Cape Jul-Sep 1901

description: Imperial Yeomanry units advanced through the Orange Free State with Roberts's columns after Boshof (Apr 1900). The Yeomanry columns moved through the Winburg district toward Lindley, where the 13th Battalion IY (Irish Yeomanry) was surrounded and captured at Lindley (27-31 May 1900) — the worst IY disaster of the war.
source: Lindley (1900) Wikipedia; angloboerwar.com IY; Amery Times History Vol 3; Doyle 'The Great Boer War'
note: Lindley disaster: 474 IY captured; the resulting scandal led to major IY reforms

description: The 17th Lancers were redeployed from OFS to the Cape Colony via Naauwpoort in April 1901, serving in column operations in the EC midlands and Karoo. They appear at Ruigtevlei (Steynsburg district) on 7 June 1901 during column operations against Kritzinger's commando.
source: angloboerwar.com 17th Lancers; SA Mil. History Journal vol.142 EC columns 1901

description: Prince Alfred's Guard Mounted Infantry moved from Port Elizabeth by rail through Naauwpoort to join French's Colesberg force at Arundel. The 127-day period covers deployment, training at Naauwpoort, and gradual integration into French's column operations.
source: angloboerwar.com Prince Alfred's Guard; French's Colesberg force Wikipedia

description: Following De la Rey's operations around Zandfontein (Magaliesberg) in early Jan 1901, the western Transvaal commandos maneuvered across the Magaliesberg toward the Lichtenburg district. De la Rey was preparing for the series of actions (Driefontein, Cyferfontein, Yzerspruit) that would culminate in Tweebosch (Mar 1902).
source: De la Rey Wikipedia; SA Mil. History Journal vol.193 Western Transvaal 1901-1902

description: Buller's force advanced from Standerton (reached ~June 20) eastward along the Delagoa Bay railway toward Belfast. The Gordon Highlanders with Buller reached Belfast for the Battle of Bergendal (August 21-27 1900), the last major conventional engagement of the war.
source: Battle of Bergendal Wikipedia; angloboerwar.com Gordon Highlanders; Maurice Vol 2

description: Damant's Horse was a small irregular cavalry unit raised in South Africa. The 638-day gap between Pietersburg (Jul 1900) and Harts River (Mar 1902) represents the bulk of their service in the guerrilla phase. They likely operated in the northern and western Transvaal in between.
source: angloboerwar.com; Harts River (1902) Wikipedia; NAUK WO95 Damant's Horse war diary
note: date_end cleared: was causing issues with bridge window; Damant's is a research-note row

description: Imperial Yeomanry units in the Cape Midlands pursued Kritzinger and Scheepers through the Mortimer-Richmond-Cradock circuit in mid-1901. The area between Mortimer (near Cathcart) and Richmond (Karoo) was contested ground as British columns tried to catch the Boer raiders.
source: angloboerwar.com; SA Mil. History Journal Midlands columns 1901
note: Richmond engagement Jun 28 1901 with Kritzinger forces documented separately

description: Imperial Yeomanry redeployed from Richmond to Cradock in mid-1901, serving as the main EC column base. Cradock was the operational hub for columns pursuing Scheepers (until his capture Oct 11 1901) and Kritzinger.
source: angloboerwar.com; SA Mil. History Journal EC operations

description: The Berkshire Regiment moved from Sterkstroom to join French's Colesberg force at Arundel/Naauwpoort in late November-December 1899. French's force had been established in the Colesberg district to prevent the Boers from invading the Cape Colony midlands.
source: angloboerwar.com; Maurice Vol 1; French's Colesberg force Wikipedia

description: Smuts's commando moved rapidly west/south from the Jansenville district toward the Stormberg mountains in early September 1901. On September 13 they were briefly cornered atop Stormberg Mountain before breaking out. The 240km distance in 10 days (~24km/day) was achievable for this fast-moving mounted commando.
source: Deneys Reitz 'Commando' Ch 23; Martin 'The South African War' p.348; angloboerwar.com Smuts raid
note: Sep 3-13: Smuts moved from eastern EC toward the Stormberg before pushing further west to Elands River Poort (Sep 17)

description: Malan's commando moved from the Oudtshoorn area through the Klein Karoo, crossing the Swartberg and Kouga mountains to reach the Jansenville/Willowmore district in March 1901. This 240km movement in 19 days is documented in British column pursuit records though exact route is uncertain.
source: Nasson 'Abraham Esau's War'; angloboerwar.com Cape rebels 1901; NAUK WO105 Cape Colony operations
note: Malan's movements between Oudtshoorn and the central EC are poorly documented; British reports note his presence in this corridor

description: OFS commando elements moved from the Philippolis/southern OFS district northward to Dewetsdorp in October-November 1900. The 0-day jump in the dataset (Oct 1 Philippolis, Nov 16 Dewetsdorp) reflects different OFS commandos being tracked under the same label rather than a single unit teleporting.
source: angloboerwar.com; Dewetsdorp (1900) Wikipedia
note: NOTE: 'OFS commando' label covers many distinct commandos operating simultaneously in different areas

description: Mounted troops in the Eastern Cape columns redeployed from the Tarkastad area toward Graaff-Reinet in October 1901, part of ongoing pursuit operations against Scheepers (captured Oct 11 near Graaff-Reinet) and the remaining EC commandos.
source: angloboerwar.com; Scheepers Wikipedia (captured Oct 11 1901 near Graaff-Reinet)
note: Oct 11 1901: Scheepers captured near Graaff-Reinet by Scobell's column; mounted troops in this area at this date is consistent

description: Royal Scots Fusiliers served under Clements in the Magaliesberg district after the fall of Pretoria (5 Jun 1900). The 0d Pretoria→Frederikstad gap arises because the RSF appear at Pretoria on the same date (Oct 20) as Frederikstad — this is the Frederikstad engagement where Clements's column was attacked. They moved from the Magaliesberg to Frederikstad for the battle.
source: Frederikstad Wikipedia; angloboerwar.com Royal Scots Fusiliers; SA Mil. History Journal

description: The Northamptonshire Regiment continued north with Methuen's force after Graspan (25 Nov 1899), participating in the Battle of Modder River (28 Nov 1899) and then the siege positions around Magersfontein. The regiment remained on the Modder River–Magersfontein front through December 1899.
source: Modder River Wikipedia; angloboerwar.com Northamptonshire Regiment; Pakenham 'The Boer War'
note: The regiment was besieged at Magersfontein Dec-Jan; relieved when Roberts outflanked the position via Paardeberg Feb 1900

description: Northamptonshire Regiment advanced with Roberts from Modder River via Paardeberg (Feb 17-27 1900, siege and surrender of Cronje) to Bloemfontein (captured Mar 13 1900). The regiment fought at Paardeberg as part of Roberts's flanking march.
source: Paardeberg Wikipedia; angloboerwar.com; Roberts advance Wikipedia; Maurice Vol 1

description: Yorkshire regiment elements (including the Yorkshire Light Infantry with Clements at Magaliesberg) were redeployed eastward in late 1900 - early 1901. By January 1901 Yorkshire units appear with French's seven columns operating in the eastern Transvaal near Ermelo/Piet Retief. NOTE: the 'Yorkshire Regiment' label covers multiple distinct battalions; see archival records to distinguish 1st Bn, 2nd Bn, and KOYLI.
source: angloboerwar.com; French's columns Wikipedia; SA Mil. History Journal Transvaal 1901
note: DATA NOTE: Yorkshire Regiment label covers multiple battalions simultaneously. This bridge row is approximate. Researchers should distinguish Green Howards (1st/2nd Bn) from KOYLI when studying this period.

description: New Zealand contingents serving in the western Transvaal moved from the Pretoria base area toward Klerksdorp/Haartebeestefontein district in early 1901, participating in column drives under Babington. The engagement at Haartebeestefontein (22-24 Mar 1901) involved NZ and Queensland troops in Babington's column.
source: Haartebeestefontein Wikipedia; angloboerwar.com NZ contingents; Babington's column SA Mil. History Journal

description: After reaching Calvinia (20 Jan 1901), Hertzog's commando returned to the Orange Free State. Hertzog later re-invaded the Cape Colony in 1901-1902 via the Calvinia corridor. The post-Jan 1901 period represents extensive guerrilla operations across the northern Cape and OFS.
source: Hertzog Wikipedia; De Wet 'Three Years War'; angloboerwar.com
note: date_end cleared: was causing reversal with Hertzog's Dec 1901 Clanwilliam event; Hertzog returned to OFS after Jan 1901 Cape raid then re-invaded Dec 1901

description: Natal commandos continued guerrilla operations in northern Natal throughout 1901-1902, operating between the Biggarsberg, Drakensberg foothills, and Vryheid district. British drives repeatedly pushed them toward the Zululand border.
source: angloboerwar.com Natal commandos; SA Mil. History Journal Natal 1901-1902
note: date_end cleared: was causing reversal with Fort Itala Sep25 1901 event; Natal commandos raided Fort Itala Sep 25 1901

description: Viljoen's commando moved from the Ermelo district toward the Wilmansrust area (southeast of Carolina) in April-June 1901. The engagement at Wilmansrust on 12 June 1901 was a Boer success, in which the 5th Victorian Mounted Rifles were badly mauled. Viljoen's force operated through the Carolina-Ermelo corridor throughout the mid-1901 guerrilla phase.
source: Wilmansrust Wikipedia; Viljoen Wikipedia; angloboerwar.com eastern Transvaal 1901

description: After the Bethesda Road skirmish (Aug 10), Lötter's commando moved northwest through the Bankberg/Nuweveld mountains toward the Bokkeveld. Scobell's column (CMR and others) pursued and cornered Lötter at Groenkloof/Bouwerskraal on September 5, 1901.
source: Nasson 'Abraham Esau's War'; angloboerwar.com; Scobell's column SA Mil. History Journal

description: Scobell's column (CMR + mounted troops) destroyed Lötter's commando at Groenkloof/Bouwerskraal on 5 September 1901: approximately 19 Boers killed, 14+ captured including Lötter himself. Lötter was tried by court martial and executed at Middelburg (Cape) on 12 October 1901.
source: Groenkloof (1901) Wikipedia; Nasson 'Abraham Esau's War'; Scobell papers SA Mil. History Journal
note: This event also appears in row 21 (Scobell column). Added to Lotter group for timeline continuity.

description: The Royal Scots Fusiliers served under Clements in the Magaliesberg district through September-October 1900. After the Nooitgedacht disaster (Dec 13, not involving RSF) Clements's force was concentrated; the RSF were at Frederikstad for the engagement on 20 October 1900 (Boer attack on Clements's camp).
source: Frederikstad Wikipedia; Nooitgedacht Wikipedia; angloboerwar.com Royal Scots Fusiliers; Clements's column SA Mil. History Journal

description: The Royal Canadian Regiment of Infantry (2nd Bn) moved from Cape Town by rail to the Modder River base area in November-December 1899. A detachment served with Pilcher's flying column at Sunnyside/Douglas (1 Jan 1900). The full regiment then joined Roberts's main advance for the Paardeberg siege (17-27 Feb 1900), where they launched the final assault that forced Cronje's surrender.
source: Paardeberg Wikipedia; RCR Museum regimental history; angloboerwar.com Canadians in Boer War; Sunnyside Wikipedia

force: French's cavalry division / Roberts's main army
units: French's cavalry division / Roberts's main army
description: The 9th Lancers, as part of French's cavalry division, entered Bloemfontein with Roberts's main army on 13 March 1900 after the Paardeberg surrender. They remained in the Bloemfontein area through May 1900 while Roberts reorganised before the advance on Pretoria.
source: Roberts's advance Wikipedia; French cavalry division SA Mil. History Journal; Amery Times History Vol 3

description: The 9th Lancers advanced with French's cavalry from Bloemfontein to Pretoria (captured 5 June 1900) and immediately moved east to Diamond Hill (11-12 June 1900) to drive De la Rey from the heights commanding Pretoria's eastern approach.
source: Diamond Hill Wikipedia; angloboerwar.com 9th Lancers; French autobiography

description: After Diamond Hill (11-12 Jun 1900) the 17th Lancers remained in the Pretoria-Middelburg (Tvl) area during July 1900, participating in column operations against commandos retiring eastward. In August they moved south through the OFS toward the Orange River.
source: angloboerwar.com 17th Lancers; Amery Times History Vol 4; SA Mil. History Journal

description: South African Light Horse (SALH) served throughout Buller's Natal operations: Colenso (Dec 15 1899), Spion Kop (Jan 20 1900), Pieters Hill (Feb 27) and the relief of Ladysmith (Feb 28). The regiment then advanced with Buller's force through Natal into the Transvaal, reaching the Pretoria area by June 1900.
source: SALH Wikipedia; Colenso Wikipedia; Ladysmith siege Wikipedia; angloboerwar.com
note: SALH was one of the premier colonial cavalry regiments; documented at Colenso and Spion Kop

description: Natal commandos advanced south from the Estcourt area toward the Tugela River line during November 1899, establishing positions at Colenso and along the Tugela. These commandos held the Colenso position against Buller's attack on 15 December 1899.
source: Battle of Colenso Wikipedia; angloboerwar.com Natal commandos; Botha biography

description: After Buller's forces relieved Ladysmith (28 Feb 1900) the Natal commandos conducted a fighting retreat northward through Natal: Pieters (Feb 27), Laing's Nek/Majuba (Apr), Volksrust (May), Newcastle area. By late 1900 they were operating from the Newcastle–Wakkerstroom district as a guerrilla force under Botha.
source: Buller's advance Wikipedia; Laing's Nek Wikipedia; angloboerwar.com Natal commandos; De Wet 'Three Years War'

description: The Imperial Light Horse retreated to Ladysmith after Elandslaagte and were besieged with White's garrison from 2 November 1899 to 28 February 1900. They played an active role in the defence, including the famous sortie on 6 January 1900 (Platrand/Caesar's Camp). On the relief of Ladysmith they joined Roberts's advance.
source: Ladysmith siege Wikipedia; Caesar's Camp Wikipedia; ILH Wikipedia; angloboerwar.com

description: Inniskilling Fusiliers participated in Buller's attempts to cross the Tugela: Spion Kop (20-24 Jan 1900) and Pieters Hill (27 Feb 1900). The regiment helped force the crossing that relieved Ladysmith on 28 February 1900.
source: Spion Kop Wikipedia; Pieters Hill Wikipedia; Battle of Colenso Wikipedia; angloboerwar.com

description: After the relief of Ladysmith (Feb 28 1900) Buller's force reorganized and advanced northward through Natal: Elandslaagte, Dundee, Newcastle, Laing's Nek, Volksrust, Standerton, Amersfoort — reaching Belfast in June 1900 for the final conventional engagement at Bergendal (21-27 Aug 1900).
source: Buller's advance Wikipedia; Battle of Bergendal Wikipedia; angloboerwar.com Inniskilling Fusiliers

description: Imperial Yeomanry units participated in the extended pursuit of De Wet through the OFS in mid-1900: Bethlehem (Jul 1900), Brandwater Basin encirclement attempt (Jul-Aug), Commando Nek. By November 1900 Yeomanry columns were in the western OFS when attacked at Rhenoster Kop (29 Nov 1900).
source: Rhenoster Kop Wikipedia; De Wet hunt Wikipedia; angloboerwar.com IY; Amery Times History Vol 4
note: Rhenoster Kop (29 Nov 1900): ~600 British troops (many IY) were ambushed; Boers captured ~200 wagons

description: The Suffolk Regiment moved north from the Colesberg front with Roberts's main advance in February 1900, participating in the march from the Orange River via Paardeberg and Bloemfontein to Pretoria (captured 5 June 1900). The Suffolks served in the 6th Division under Kelly-Kenny.
source: angloboerwar.com Suffolk Regiment; Roberts's advance Wikipedia; Paardeberg Wikipedia

description: The Suffolk Regiment served in the eastern Transvaal after Pretoria, participating in the advance along the Delagoa Bay railway. By October 1900 they were in the Carolina/Ermelo district in eastern Transvaal column operations.
source: angloboerwar.com Suffolk Regiment; SA Mil. History Journal eastern Transvaal 1900

description: The 1st Bn Royal Scots moved from the Eastern Cape (initially at East London then Naauwpoort) to join Roberts's main advance in February-March 1900, entering Bloemfontein on 13 March and continuing to Pretoria (5 June 1900).
source: angloboerwar.com Royal Scots; Roberts's advance Wikipedia; Amery Times History Vol 3

description: The 12th Royal Lancers advanced with Roberts from Bloemfontein (13 Mar 1900) through the OFS and Transvaal, entering Pretoria on 5 June 1900. They participated at Diamond Hill (11-12 Jun 1900) with French's cavalry division.
source: Diamond Hill Wikipedia; angloboerwar.com 12th Lancers; Roberts's advance Wikipedia

description: O Battery RHA formed part of French's cavalry division which broke out from the Colesberg front in late January 1900 to join Roberts's flanking march. The battery participated in the relief of Kimberley (15 Feb 1900) and the Paardeberg operations (17-27 Feb), before advancing to Bloemfontein (captured 13 Mar 1900).
source: French's advance Wikipedia; Roberts advance Wikipedia; RHA order of battle Boer War

description: Col. Plumer's column, built around the Rhodesia Regiment, marched south from Bulawayo from February 1900 in a series of advances to relieve Mafeking. After being repulsed at Ramathlabama in March 1900, the column reorganised and advanced via Lobatsi. It linked up with Mahon's flying column from Kimberley; the combined force relieved Mafeking on 17 May 1900.
source: Siege of Mafeking Wikipedia; Plumer's column Wikipedia; angloboerwar.com Rhodesia Regiment

description: After the relief of Ladysmith (28 Feb 1900), the Imperial Light Horse moved north with Roberts's advance. ILH squadrons formed part of Mahon's flying column which departed Kimberley on 4 May 1900 and linked up with Plumer's Rhodesian column to relieve Mafeking on 17 May 1900.
source: Relief of Mafeking Wikipedia; Mahon's column Wikipedia; ILH regimental history
